layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts near the top hero where the devotional header sits over a warm, spiritual background image. It is first drawn to the large, serif devotional title and any subtle iconography (e.g., cross or ministry mark), then moves down to the centered scripture reference or key verse positioned in the mid-height of the hero. From there, the user’s attention flows naturally into the main content viewer just below the hero, continuing down through quiet utility actions (share/print, reading list toggle state) and finally to the adjacent navigation area at the bottom. When present, overlays like loader, error, or reading list prompts temporarily become the focal point by dimming the background and centering their content, then return focus to the main vertical flow when dismissed.

layout.overall_description: The Devotional Detail page opens with a full-width hero band at the top, directly beneath the site’s global navigation. This hero mirrors the original mockup: a warm, sunset-toned photograph fills the background edge to edge, darkening slightly toward the center to support legible text. In the middle of this image sits a single, centered column of typography—the devotional’s title in an elegant serif, a restrained line of metadata, and a key scripture reference or short verse—stacked vertically with generous breathing room so that no text touches the edges of the hero. Below this, the photographic background gives way to a calm, off-white reading surface where the devotional content flows in a single column, comfortably wide but still intimate. Just above or alongside the first lines of the content, subtle controls for saving to a reading list and for sharing/printing appear, aligned to the same central column but visually lighter than the text. As the reader scrolls down, the devotional text remains the clear focus, framed by generous vertical spacing. Finally, near the bottom of the page, a spacious adjacent navigation section presents links to the previous and next devotionals, aligned with the main column and given room to breathe. When loading, on error, or when prompting for reading-list actions, a centered overlay softly dims the background and places a simple, reverent message in the middle of the screen, always returning the user gracefully to the main vertical flow once resolved.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a full-viewport overlay that sits above the hero and all underlying content. It uses a semi-transparent, warm-toned scrim that subtly darkens the photographic background and page canvas while still allowing their shapes to be faintly visible, preserving context. At the exact center of the viewport—both horizontally and vertically—sits a small, unobtrusive loading indicator (spinner or subtle cross-shaped progress icon) with a short line of text beneath it. The loader spans 100% of the width and height of the browser window and aligns with the spiritual, reverent tone by avoiding harsh animations. When active, it visually eclipses other components but does not change their layout; they remain in place beneath the overlay.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay covering the entire viewport, centered content; appears above all other components and scrolls with the page content locked in place (page scroll disabled while active).

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The devotional header is a hero-style section that sits at the very top of the page content, directly beneath the global navigation bar. It spans the full width of the browser and occupies approximately 45–55% of the initial viewport height on desktop (with a minimum height of ~320–400px), using a full-bleed background image in warm sunset tones. Over this image, a subtle vertical gradient darkens the middle third of the hero so that text remains readable without losing the photograph’s presence. Centered within this hero is a single column whose width is capped at about 720–800px; this column is horizontally centered and vertically aligned slightly above the precise middle of the hero, echoing the mockup’s upper 20–25% for title and metadata and central 40–50% for scripture. Within this column, content is stacked: first a fine, small sans-serif line of metadata (e.g., date, category) at the top, then the devotional title in large, uppercase or small caps serif, centered and spanning roughly 60–80% of the column width, and finally a key scripture reference or short verse centered beneath the title. Vertical spacing follows the 8px scale, with 24–32px separating each text block and 48–64px of empty space above the metadata and below the scripture to keep the hero airy. No other controls or dense UI occupy this hero; it mirrors the mockup’s simplicity with only navigation (global), title, and scripture visible against the image.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static full-width hero section at the top of the page content; does not scroll independently but moves as part of the normal page scroll.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The devotional content viewer sits immediately below the devotional header, separated by a comfortable band of whitespace roughly 64px tall, allowing a clear visual transition from image-based hero to text-based body. The content viewer is centered on the page and constrained to a single column that matches or is slightly narrower than the header’s text column (approximately 640–720px wide), reinforcing a consistent central axis. It rests on a solid, light background (white or slightly warm off-white) with 24–32px of inner padding on all sides to create an airy reading frame around the text. The paragraphs flow in a linear top-to-bottom sequence, with generous line spacing and 24px vertical spacing between logical sections or subheadings. On larger screens, there is substantial left and right margin (at least 15–20% of the viewport each side) so that the reading column appears as a focused, devotional canvas in the middle of the screen. The top of the content viewer aligns visually with any utility bar or controls above it, so that the first line of body text feels anchored under the hero and any actions.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static central content column that scrolls with the page; no independent scrolling within this component to keep the reading experience continuous.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading list toggle is a small, focused control positioned near the top of the main content area but visually tied to the header’s central axis. On desktop, it appears in the first horizontal row below the hero, aligned to the right edge of the content column that contains the devotional text. It sits above the first paragraph of the devotional content and slightly below the visual base of the hero (about 24–32px beneath the hero-content gap), giving the sense that it belongs to the devotional as a whole without intruding on the hero’s clean typography. The toggle itself uses a compact icon (e.g., bookmark or heart) with a short label, matching the neutral sans-serif used for scripture and metadata, and occupies only a small rectangle (roughly the height of a single line of body text, with 8–16px padding). The left side of this same horizontal row remains mostly empty, preserving the open, centered aesthetic. On narrower screens, the toggle shifts to a centered position directly beneath the header content and above the main text, becoming a single, centered line with generous top and bottom spacing (16–24px) to maintain balance.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline with the top of the content viewer; its position is anchored relative to the devotional content column and scrolls normally with the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading list prompt appears only when the user interacts with the reading list toggle and additional confirmation or sign-in is required. It manifests as a centered modal overlay that gently dims the entire page with a soft, semi-transparent background scrim, similar to the loader but lighter and more subtle. The modal itself is a vertically oriented card placed at the exact horizontal center of the viewport and slightly above the vertical center, echoing the vertical bias of the hero’s text. It spans roughly 360–480px in width on desktop (padding 24–32px inside) and uses rounded corners and a neutral light background, with text and buttons set in the same sans-serif used in the body. Content inside is stacked: a brief title line at the top, 1–3 short lines of explanatory copy, then primary and secondary buttons centered beneath with 16–24px spacing. On mobile, the same component expands to use ~90% of the viewport width and sits closer to mid-height, maintaining generous spacing from edges. The underlying hero and content remain visible but out of focus, reinforcing that this is a temporary interruption that still respects the devotional context.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed, centered modal overlay with dimmed background; appears above all page content and locks page scroll while active.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The adjacent navigation sits at the bottom of the devotional content, serving as a gentle footer-like section within the same central column. It is separated from the last line of the devotional by a generous vertical gap of about 64–96px to clearly signal the end of the reading. The component aligns with the main content width (approximately 640–720px) and is centered on the page. Within this area, two main navigation items—typically previous and next devotionals—are laid out horizontally: the 'Previous' devotional is left-aligned within the column and the 'Next' devotional is right-aligned, creating a subtle left-right tension while the whole block itself remains centered. Each item is represented by a simple text label (and optional small arrow icon), kept light in weight to avoid overpowering the devotional text. Ample padding (24–32px vertically) surrounds these links, and there is additional whitespace below them (48–64px) before the end of the page content or footer, echoing the open, contemplative spacing from the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static section at the bottom of the main content flow; scrolls naturally with the page and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The share and print bar is a slim utility row that appears just above the devotional content viewer, occupying the same width as the main content column and centered on the page. It sits between the hero and the first paragraph of the devotional, typically directly below the reading list toggle, with 16–24px vertical spacing separating each element so that the transition from hero to text remains clean. The bar itself is visually understated: a horizontal strip with no heavy background, or at most a very light tint, containing a small cluster of icon-and-label buttons (for sharing and printing) grouped to the right side on desktop. The left portion of the bar remains mostly open, preserving the sense of space. The height of the bar is modest—roughly the height of one to one-and-a-half lines of text plus 8–12px padding above and below—so it reads as a light touch, not a thick toolbar. On smaller screens, the icons reflow into a single centered row, and the bar retains generous top and bottom margins so that it visually separates from both hero and body text without crowding either.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline utility row attached to the top of the content viewer; scrolls with the page and does not remain fixed.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: When an error occurs loading the devotional, the error state occupies the same general region as the hero and top portion of the content, becoming the primary visual element. Instead of the photographic hero, the page shows a calm, solid or softly textured background covering the full width of the viewport from just beneath the global navigation to roughly mid-screen. Centered within this area is a compact error card or message block aligned to the same central column used by the header. The error message is vertically centered within this region, with a simple icon (such as a subtle warning symbol or cross) at the top, a concise heading beneath, and 1–2 lines of explanatory text below that. Optional retry or navigation actions are centered underneath with ample vertical spacing (16–24px). Generous empty space surrounds the card on all sides, and no other devotional content is shown beneath it, keeping the page visually quiet and focused on the message. The layout maintains the same single-column, centered structure as the normal state, so users feel continuity even in error.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static centered block that replaces the usual hero and content areas when present; scrollable only if content exceeds viewport height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The document title manager has no visible footprint on the page. Conceptually, it aligns with the devotional header: it updates the browser tab title and other metadata to reflect the devotional’s title and key scripture, mirroring the way that title is visually presented in the hero. It operates in the background, synchronized with the header’s content, but does not add or change any on-screen layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, meta-level component; does not occupy any spatial area in the layout.

name: Reading List
title: Reading List
description: Manage your saved devotionals: view, filter by status, open details, mark as read, and remove items from your personal reading list.

core_features: Fetch and display the user's devotional reading list using the getReadingListItems interface.
Filter visible reading list items by status (e.g., saved or read) using the URL status parameter and client-side filtering.
Open a devotional's detail page by clicking its title, navigating to devotional_detail.html with the devotional ID in the URL.
Mark individual reading list items as read or saved using updateReadingListItemStatus.
Remove a devotional from the reading list using removeDevotionalFromReadingList and reflect that change immediately in the UI.
Optionally organize the list via status filtering and client-side sorting (e.g., by date added or date published).
Provide a direct navigation path to devotionals.html to discover and add more devotionals when the list is empty or when the user wants additional content.
Handle loading and error states for the getReadingListItems, updateReadingListItemStatus, and removeDevotionalFromReadingList interfaces.
user_workflows:
View and filter reading list
1) User opens reading_list.html (via footer link or direct URL).
2) On page load, the page controller reads the status URL parameter (if present).
3) The page controller calls getReadingListItems with no parameters.
4) When the response returns, the list name and items are stored in page state.
5) The status filter uses the status URL parameter to set the initial filter selection.
6) The list of items is filtered client-side based on the selected status (or shows all if no status is set or if the filter is set to a general option).
7) The filtered items are rendered with title, excerpt, topic tags, reading time, date published, status, and added_at for each item.

Open devotional details from reading list
1) User scans the list and clicks on a devotional title.
2) The click handler reads the devotional_id associated with the clicked reading_list_item_id from the currently loaded data.
3) The browser navigates to devotional_detail.html?id={devotional_id} using standard link navigation.
4) The reading list page does not need to make additional API calls for this navigation; it relies on the already loaded devotional_id from getReadingListItems.

Mark an item as read or saved
1) For a given reading list item, the user clicks a control (such as a toggle or menu) to change its status (e.g., from saved to read, or from read back to saved).
2) The item component determines the target status based on the current status.
3) The item component calls updateReadingListItemStatus with parameters (readingListItemId, status), where readingListItemId is the item's reading_list_item_id and status is the new status value.
4) While waiting for the response, the UI may show a loading indicator for that item and optionally optimistically update the status in local state.
5) On success, the item status in page state is updated to the returned updated_status and any success message may be used for inline feedback.
6) If the user is currently filtered by a specific status, the item may move in or out of the filtered view based on the new status.
7) On error, the status is reverted in page state and an error message is displayed.

Remove a devotional from the reading list
1) For a given reading list item, the user clicks a 'Remove' control.
2) Optionally, a confirmation step is shown in the UI; if the user confirms, the removal proceeds.
3) The page calls removeDevotionalFromReadingList with devotionalId set to the devotional_id associated with the reading list item.
4) While waiting for the response, the item may be marked as pending removal in the UI.
5) On success, the item is removed from the in-memory list of items and the displayed list re-renders without that item; the returned total_saved_count can be used in summary components as needed.
6) If the list becomes empty, the empty-state component is shown with a call-to-action to visit devotionals.html.
7) On error, the item remains in the list and an error message is displayed.

Change status filter and update URL
1) User clicks a filter option (e.g., All, Saved, Read) in the filter bar.
2) The filter component updates the status selection in page state.
3) The page controller updates the browser URL query string to include or update the status parameter: reading_list.html?status={selectedStatus} (or removes it when showing all statuses).
4) The list of items is re-filtered client-side based on the new selectedStatus.
5) If the user navigates via the browser back/forward buttons, the controller reads the updated status parameter and synchronizes the filter and list with the URL state.

Sort reading list items
1) User selects a sort option from the sort controls (for example, by date added or by date published, ascending or descending).
2) The sort component updates the sort criteria in page state.
3) The page controller reorders the currently filtered list of items in memory according to the chosen criteria.
4) The list component re-renders to reflect the new order without additional API calls.
5) Optional: the selected sort option can be reflected in the URL as a query parameter (e.g., sort=added_at_desc) if desired for deep-linking, but all data still comes from the original getReadingListItems response.

Find more devotionals when list is empty or user wants more content
1) If getReadingListItems returns an empty items array, the empty-state component is displayed instead of the list.
2) The user clicks a 'Find more devotionals' control.
3) The browser navigates to devotionals.html with no additional parameters, as defined in the outgoing connections.
4) When the user later adds devotionals to their reading list from other pages and returns to reading_list.html, getReadingListItems is called again and the list is populated.
interactions: Click on a devotional title to navigate to devotional_detail.html?id={devotional_id}.
Change the status filter (e.g., via buttons or a dropdown) to show items for a specific status; this updates the URL status parameter and triggers client-side filtering.
Click a control on an item to mark it as read; this calls updateReadingListItemStatus with status set to a read value and updates the UI when the call succeeds.
Click a control on an item to mark it as saved (if it was previously read); this calls updateReadingListItemStatus with status set to a saved value.
Click a 'Remove' control on an item; after optional confirmation, this calls removeDevotionalFromReadingList with the devotional_id and removes the item from the list upon success.
Use sort controls (e.g., dropdown or buttons) to change how items are ordered in the list; this adjusts the order of items client-side without extra API calls.
Click a 'Find more devotionals' button or link to navigate to devotionals.html.
On page load, the page automatically calls getReadingListItems to retrieve current data and renders loading and error states appropriately.
When browser navigation changes the status parameter (via back/forward), the page controller re-reads the URL and updates the filter and list to stay in sync.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary shared state between the URL and the page is the status query parameter. On initial load, the page controller reads window.location.search to parse the status parameter, if present. Accepted status values correspond to those provided in ReadingListItem.status and the incoming_params definition (e.g., 'saved' or 'read'); any unsupported value is treated as 'all' (no filter). The controller then calls getReadingListItems with no parameters and receives reading_list_name and items. The full items collection is stored in page state. A derived filteredItems collection is computed based on the current status filter: if status is not set or represents 'all', filteredItems equals items; if status is a valid status string, filteredItems includes only items where item.status matches the selected status. Sorting is applied to filteredItems according to the sort state. The status filter UI is initialized based on the status URL parameter, so deep-linked URLs (e.g., reading_list.html?status=read) show the appropriate view immediately. When the user changes the filter, the controller updates both the in-memory status state and the URL query string via navigation or history API. The page does not use localStorage or other persistent client storage; all persistent data comes from the assigned interfaces. Updates to item status are made via updateReadingListItemStatus, which returns updated_status; the matching item in page state is updated accordingly. If the current status filter excludes the new status, the item is removed from filteredItems in the rendered list. Removing items uses removeDevotionalFromReadingList, keyed by devotionalId; on success the corresponding item is removed from items in page state. The total_saved_count returned from this interface can be used by summary components to display up-to-date counts without requiring another call to getReadingListItems, but it does not replace the main in-memory list. Error states for any interface are stored in transient error state fields and displayed through dedicated error components without changing URL parameters.

components.functionality: Coordinates data fetching, URL parameter parsing, high-level state management, and communication between child components on reading_list.html. On mount, it reads the status query parameter, calls getReadingListItems, stores reading_list_name and items in state, derives filtered and sorted views, and passes relevant data and callbacks down. It also updates the URL when filters change and handles responses from updateReadingListItemStatus and removeDevotionalFromReadingList.

Displays the reading list name from the API response and optionally a dynamic summary such as the total number of items and counts by status, derived from the current items array. It does not hardcode any counts or text beyond static labels; all numbers come from the live data.

Allows the user to filter the reading list by item status using controls such as buttons or a dropdown. It initializes its selected value from the status URL parameter provided by the page controller. When the user changes the filter, it notifies the page controller, which updates the URL status parameter and recalculates the filteredItems collection.

Provides options to sort the currently filtered reading list items by different fields (for example, added_at or date_published, and possibly ascending/descending). It does not fetch new data; instead, it communicates the chosen sort criteria to the page controller, which reorders the in-memory filteredItems collection.

Renders the collection of filtered and sorted reading list items. It receives an array of items from the page controller and renders one reading-list-item-row component per item. If the array is empty, it does not render rows and instead allows the empty state component to be shown.

Represents a single devotional in the reading list. Displays the devotional title, excerpt, topic tags, reading time, date published, current status, and the date it was added. Provides controls to open the devotional detail page, change status (read/saved), and remove the devotional from the reading list. It delegates status updates and removal to the page controller, which then calls updateReadingListItemStatus or removeDevotionalFromReadingList.

Displays when getReadingListItems returns an empty items array or when all items have been removed. Shows explanatory text and a 'Find more devotionals' action that navigates to devotionals.html. It relies solely on the live items length from the page controller and does not contain any hardcoded counts or content-specific examples.

Shows a loading state while getReadingListItems, updateReadingListItemStatus, or removeDevotionalFromReadingList calls are in progress. The page controller toggles a loading flag before and after each API call, and this component renders accordingly.

Displays error messages returned from getReadingListItems, updateReadingListItemStatus, or removeDevotionalFromReadingList. It takes generic error strings or the message property from the updateReadingListItemStatus and removeDevotionalFromReadingList responses and presents them without hardcoding any specific error content.

Provides a persistent 'Find more devotionals' action and any other list-level navigation relevant to the reading list. The 'Find more devotionals' action navigates to devotionals.html according to the outgoing_connections definition.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single central column likely mapped to a standard 12-column grid, with all visible content centered within a full-width hero section.
layout.layout_pattern: All content is arranged in a single, vertically stacked central column that sits within a full-width, full-height background (continuing the hero-image feel). The page-controller defines this centered column; within it, the reading-list-header sits near the top, followed by a compact horizontal band containing the status filter bar and sort controls, then the list/empty/loading/error content in the middle, and finally footer actions near the bottom. There are no sidebars; every component is centered and spans a restrained maximum width to keep the devotional, focused feeling.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All content is arranged in a single, vertically stacked central column that sits within a full-width, full-height background (continuing the hero-image feel). The page-controller defines this centered column; within it, the reading-list-header sits near the top, followed by a compact horizontal band containing the status filter bar and sort controls, then the list/empty/loading/error content in the middle, and finally footer actions near the bottom. There are no sidebars; every component is centered and spans a restrained maximum width to keep the devotional, focused feeling.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The upper 20–25% of the viewport is devoted to the reading-list-header and any immediate error banner, echoing the hero’s top-weighted title zone. The central 40–50% is reserved for the actual reading list content (or empty/loading states), visually sitting over the darker midsection of the background so text is legible. The lower remaining area is left mostly open, with the footer actions anchored near the bottom of the content and framed by generous vertical whitespace and unobstructed imagery, preserving the airy, contemplative atmosphere. Horizontal space is constrained to a central column taking about 70–80% of the viewport width, capped at a max-width (e.g., 960–1040px) with large margins on the left and right.
layout_strategies.content_density: The page uses low content density, mirroring the hero: few, clearly separated blocks with generous vertical spacing (64–96px between major sections, 24–32px padding within each block). Rows in the list are clearly separated, with each item given breathing room instead of tight tabular packing. Controls (filters, sorting, footer actions) are lightweight and minimally intrusive, grouped in slim bands rather than heavy panels. The overall feeling is calm and uncluttered, emphasizing spiritual reflection over data density.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye moves from top to bottom in a deliberate, unhurried flow: starting at the reading-list-header (styled like a subdued hero heading), then naturally dropping to the horizontal band of filters and sorting, then into the main list area where individual devotionals are scanned. Any error or loading indicators appear just below the header so they’re encountered early without disrupting the column’s flow. Finally, the footer actions at the bottom act as a soft visual endpoint, framed by open space and the lighter area of the background image, echoing the hero’s transition from dense central content to more open image at the edges.

layout.overall_description: The Reading List page appears as a serene, full-height section set over a soft, warm-toned photographic background that echoes the hero image from the landing page. At the center, a single vertical column of content is visually anchored, floating slightly above the background with subtle contrast, while the left and right edges remain mostly empty, letting the image breathe. Near the top of this column, a reverent, serif-based reading list header introduces the page and summarizes your saved devotionals. Beneath it, a slim, centered control band combines status filters and sorting in a single horizontal strip, like a quiet sub-navigation line. As you move down, the central portion of the screen is dedicated to your reading list: devotionals appear as softly separated rows or cards, each given generous vertical space and centered alignment. When there are no items or the list is loading, a single, well-spaced panel occupies this central zone, much like the scripture block in the hero. An unobtrusive error banner, when present, slots just under the header without breaking the vertical rhythm. At the bottom of the content, a set of footer actions is centered and spaced away from the list, resting in a calmer area of the background, creating a gentle landing point for the eye and reinforcing the page’s devotional, contemplative character.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller acts as the invisible frame for the entire Reading List view. It occupies the full viewport width and at least the full viewport height, sitting directly beneath the global site navigation and over the full-bleed background image or gradient. Inside, it defines a centered content column that spans approximately 70–80% of the viewport width, capped at a max-width of around 960–1040px, with symmetrical left and right margins. Top padding of about 64–96px separates the reading-list-header from the global navigation, and bottom padding of similar scale ensures the footer actions and final rows never touch the viewport edge. All other components—header, filter bar, sort controls, list/empty/loading/error, and footer actions—are vertically stacked within this central column with consistent 24–32px internal padding and 32–64px vertical gaps, maintaining the open, devotional layout seen in the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard document flow container; not visible as a distinct element but establishes a centered, single-column layout with full-height behavior and responsive width constraints.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-header is positioned at the top of the central column, just below any site-level nav, functioning as a subdued hero for this page. It spans the full width of the central column (about 70–80% of the viewport, up to ~960–1040px) and is horizontally centered. Vertically, it occupies roughly the top 20–25% of the initial viewport height, with generous top padding (48–64px) from the global nav and bottom margin of around 32–48px before the filter/sort band. The main title is set in an elegant serif, centered, potentially in uppercase or small caps to echo the brand statement from the original hero, with a small cross or subtle icon aligned either above or to the left of the title but kept within the centered column. Below the title, a short summary line (e.g., count of saved devotionals or a brief scripture snippet) is centered in a neutral sans-serif, spaced about 16–24px beneath the title. The header background remains transparent, letting the hero-style background image show through; legibility is ensured via a subtle text shadow or a soft translucent overlay behind the text confined to the header zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrolls with the page; visually anchors the top portion of the reading list page without using sticky behavior.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The status filter bar sits directly below the reading-list-header within the central column, with a vertical gap of about 24–32px to maintain separation while preserving the linear flow. It forms the left half or upper line of a slim horizontal control band that shares visual space with the sort controls. The entire band is centered within the column and occupies its full width, but individual filter pills or buttons are horizontally centered or slightly left-biased within that band, maintaining overall central alignment. The bar height is modest (around 48px), with 12–16px inner padding above and below the controls to keep it light. Filters are arranged in a single row with equal spacing, but the row width doesn’t exceed the content; there are comfortable side margins (at least 24px) before reaching the edges of the central column. The background may be slightly more opaque than the hero image—such as a very soft, translucent panel—to provide contrast while still feeling airy, and the bar is visually subordinate to the header but clearly connected, like a calm sub-navigation line.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows in the normal document order. Optionally can become sticky at the top of the viewport once scrolled past the header on larger screens, but if sticky is used, it should remain a slim, unobtrusive strip that does not disrupt the meditative feel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort controls are visually grouped with the status filter bar to create a single, cohesive control band just below the header. Within the central column, they align horizontally on the same baseline as the filters or directly beneath them if space is constrained on smaller screens. On larger screens, the sort dropdown or buttons sit on the right side of this band while the filters remain toward the left, but the entire band itself is still centered within the page’s central column, maintaining the single-column pattern. Vertical spacing mirrors the filter bar, with 12–16px of top and bottom padding, such that the combined band height is around 48–56px. On smaller devices, the sort controls stack below the filters with a 8–16px gap, still centered. The sort controls use neutral sans-serif text and a light outline or understated caret icon, visually subordinate to the header and visually on par with the filters. A 24–32px margin below this combined control band separates it from the main list area, creating a clear transition into the content zone.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline with the filter bar as part of a shared horizontal band, participating in any sticky behavior that the filter bar might adopt, otherwise scrolling normally.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reading-list-list occupies the central portion of the page below the filter/sort band, beginning after a vertical margin of about 32–40px to give a sense of transition from controls to content. It spans the same central column width (70–80% of the viewport, max ~960–1040px) and is horizontally centered. Vertically, this list region is expected to fill roughly 40–50% of the viewport height on initial load, aligning with the darker, more contrast-rich zone of the background image to keep the text readable. The list is composed of vertically stacked item rows, each separated by 16–24px of vertical space, ensuring that no row feels cramped. The list container itself has 16–24px of top and bottom padding, so the first and last row don’t touch the surrounding components. When long, the list simply extends downward, with consistent spacing between rows, while the top controls and header scroll gracefully out of view. The edges of the list maintain clear left and right padding relative to the column edges, preserving the sense of open margins.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard vertically scrollable list within the central column; it grows with content and defines the main scrolling area of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each reading-list-item-row appears as a soft, card-like strip centered within the reading-list-list. It stretches across most of the central column—about 90–100% of the column width—with 16–24px of inner padding on all sides. The row height is flexible, typically around 72–120px depending on content, remaining relatively low-density. Text and actions inside follow a horizontal pattern: title and key metadata are left-aligned within the row, while primary actions (open details, mark as read) sit toward the right, but the entire row remains within the centered central column, preserving the single-column layout. The background is slightly more opaque or subtly contrasted against the hero background (e.g., a semi-translucent dark or light panel) to ensure readability without feeling heavy. Rounded corners and a faint shadow or border help each row feel distinct but gentle. Between rows, 16–24px of vertical space leaves a visible gap so each devotional feels separate and important, echoing the intentional spacing of scripture lines in the hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained within the list; rows do not stick or float. Hover or focus states may slightly elevate or brighten the row but keep it firmly within the centered stack.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state panel replaces the reading-list-list when there are no items, occupying the same central column position and width. It is vertically positioned so that its core content (icon, title, and message) sits in the central 40–50% of the viewport height, mirroring the placement of the scripture text in the hero image. The panel is centered horizontally, with a max-width narrower than the list—about 60–70% of the column width—to create a focused, contemplative island of content. Inside the panel, an icon or simple illustration sits at the top center, with a generous margin (16–24px) to a title in serif or semi-serif, followed by a short message and perhaps a devotional hint or verse in a sans-serif style, all centered. The panel uses 24–32px of internal padding and has sufficient contrast with the background (a soft translucent overlay) to feel like a calm, spiritual message rather than a technical absence. A call-to-action button, if present, is centered at the bottom of the panel with at least 24px of spacing above and below, and the area around the panel remains largely unobstructed, letting the background image frame the message.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears only when the list is empty, occupying the list’s spatial slot. Scrolls with the page but is typically fully visible without scrolling on standard viewports.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loading indicator appears in place of the list or empty state, positioned in the same central area of the page. It is horizontally centered within the central column and vertically aligned near the mid-height of the viewport, roughly in the middle 40–50% band, similar to the scripture block in the hero. The indicator (spinner, subtle animation, or progress text) is surrounded by generous whitespace: at least 32px of padding within any container and clear separation from the filter/sort controls above and footer actions below. There is no heavy panel; instead, the indicator sits lightly over the background, with only minimal contrast treatment (e.g., a small translucent disc behind a spinner) to keep the focus gentle and unobtrusive. Short loading text, if used, is centered beneath the spinner with 8–12px spacing, in a neutral sans-serif that does not compete with page headings.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily occupies the list’s visual slot during data loading. It does not fix to the viewport; it scrolls with the rest of the content but is typically initially visible without scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The error banner sits within the central column just below the reading-list-header and above the filter/sort band, ensuring it is encountered early in the vertical flow without overshadowing the page title. It spans the full width of the central column but is relatively low in height—around 48–64px—with 12–16px of vertical padding to keep it slim and respectful. The banner background is more opaque and uses a muted, warm warning tone that harmonizes with the sunset palette, avoiding harsh, saturated reds. Error text is left-aligned within the banner but the banner itself remains centered in the column, and any dismiss or retry actions sit to the right within the same band. A margin of around 16–24px separates the banner from both the header above and the controls below, so it feels like a clear but not jarring interruption. When no error is present, this space collapses, preserving the clean header-to-controls transition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears dynamically within the normal document flow. It is not sticky; it scrolls away with its surrounding content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The footer actions are positioned at the bottom of the central content column, below the list (or empty/loading state), with a generous vertical margin of about 48–64px separating them from the last item or panel. They are horizontally centered and typically occupy no more than 50–60% of the central column width, presented as one or two primary actions (for example, clear list or navigate to more devotionals) arranged either on a single centered row or stacked vertically with 16–24px between buttons. The area below the footer actions to the bottom edge of the viewport remains relatively open, allowing the background image’s lighter lower gradient to show through and give a sense of visual release. The footer actions thus act as a soft visual endpoint to the page, echoing the space beneath the scripture text in the hero. Buttons are visually clear but not oversized, with calm, rounded shapes and enough surrounding whitespace (24–32px around the button group) to feel unhurried.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Anchored to the bottom of the content rather than the viewport; they scroll with the page and are not fixed, ensuring the page retains a natural, document-like scroll.

name: Sunday Services
title: Sunday Services
description: Displays upcoming Sunday services, highlights the next or selected service, and provides navigation to reserve seats for a chosen service.

core_features: Fetch upcoming SundayService records using getUpcomingSundayServices, starting from the current date.
Highlight a primary service: either the service specified by the serviceId URL parameter (if present and in the result set) or the next_upcoming_service_id returned by getUpcomingSundayServices.
Display a list of upcoming Sunday services with key details from SundayService (date, time, location, series, childcare availability, status, and basic capacity/availability).
Provide a Reserve Seats action for each service that is reservable (e.g., status scheduled and not at capacity), navigating to service_reservation.html with the corresponding serviceId.
Indicate non-reservable services (e.g., cancelled, completed, or full) and disable or remove the Reserve Seats action for those services.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: View the next upcoming service and reserve seats
1. User navigates to sunday_services.html without any URL parameters.
2. The page calls getUpcomingSundayServices with fromDate set to the current date/time and maxCount derived from page configuration or URL parameters.
3. The response provides an array of SundayService records and next_upcoming_service_id.
4. The page sets the highlighted service to the service whose id matches next_upcoming_service_id.
5. The highlighted service panel shows the date, time (using start_time_label or start_datetime), location_name, service_series/title, childcare_available, and availability derived from capacity and reserved_seats.
6. User clicks the Reserve Seats button in the highlighted panel.
7. The page navigates to service_reservation.html?serviceId={id of the highlighted SundayService}.
8. After completing or cancelling the reservation, service_reservation.html can send the user back to sunday_services.html (optionally including serviceId in the URL), where the page re-runs the same logic and keeps that service highlighted.

Workflow 2: Return from home or reservation page with a specific service selected
1. User comes from index.html or service_reservation.html via a link like sunday_services.html?serviceId={someId}.
2. On load, the page reads the serviceId parameter from the URL.
3. The page calls getUpcomingSundayServices using fromDate (current date/time or a date derived from other URL parameters) and maxCount.
4. Once services are loaded, the page looks for a service whose id matches the serviceId URL parameter.
5. If found, that service becomes the highlighted service, even if it is different from next_upcoming_service_id.
6. The service row in the list is visually marked as selected, and the highlighted panel shows its details and Reserve Seats button (if reservable).
7. If the serviceId from the URL does not match any returned SundayService, the page falls back to highlighting the service with id equal to next_upcoming_service_id (if present) and clears any invalid selection state.

Workflow 3: Scan the list for a different service and reserve seats
1. User lands on sunday_services.html (with or without serviceId in the URL) and sees the list of upcoming services sorted by start_datetime ascending.
2. User scrolls or navigates within the service list to find a preferred date/time.
3. User clicks anywhere on a specific service row (or a dedicated Select/Details control) to set that service as the highlighted service.
4. The page updates internal state to selectedServiceId and optionally updates the URL (e.g., replacing or appending serviceId with the selected service id).
5. The highlighted service panel updates to show the details of the newly selected service.
6. User clicks the Reserve Seats button associated with the chosen service (either on the row or in the highlighted panel).
7. The page navigates to service_reservation.html?serviceId={id of the chosen SundayService}.
interactions:
Page load and data fetch:
- Event: Page initialization.
- Action: Read serviceId and optional control parameters (such as maxCount or fromDate override) from the URL.
- Action: Invoke getUpcomingSundayServices with fromDate and maxCount.
- Action: Store the returned services array and next_upcoming_service_id in page state.
- Action: Determine initial highlighted service based on state_logic.

Selecting a service from the list:
- Event: User clicks on a service row in the services list (excluding the Reserve Seats button).
- Action: Set selectedServiceId in page state to the clicked service's id.
- Action: Optionally update the URL query string serviceId to the selected service's id (using history.replaceState or a full reload, depending on implementation).
- Action: Update the highlighted panel to display the newly selected service's details.
- Action: Visually mark the selected service row as active in the list.

Triggering a reservation from the highlighted panel:
- Event: User clicks the Reserve Seats button in the highlighted service panel.
- Precondition: The highlighted service is reservable (e.g., status is scheduled and capacity permits more reservations).
- Action: Navigate to service_reservation.html?serviceId={id of the highlighted SundayService}.

Triggering a reservation from a list item:
- Event: User clicks the Reserve Seats button within a service row in the list.
- Precondition: That service is reservable.
- Action: Navigate directly to service_reservation.html?serviceId={id of that SundayService}, regardless of which service is currently highlighted.

Handling non-reservable services:
- Event: Page renders a SundayService whose status or capacity makes it non-reservable.
- Action: Display a non-interactive status indicator (e.g., cancelled, completed, or full) instead of an active Reserve Seats button.
- Action: Ensure clicking the row still allows selection for viewing details, but does not navigate to service_reservation.html when the reservation action is disabled.

Resetting to next upcoming service:
- Event: User clicks a Clear Selection or Reset to Next Upcoming control in the highlighted panel.
- Action: Clear selectedServiceId from page state and URL (remove serviceId parameter).
- Action: If next_upcoming_service_id exists in page state and matches a service in the services array, set selectedServiceId to that id.
- Action: Update both highlighted panel and list selection state accordingly.

Refreshing or changing the range of upcoming services:
- Event: User interacts with optional controls such as a date-range selector or a "Load more upcoming services" control.
- Action: Update fromDate and/or maxCount in page state and URL.
- Action: Re-invoke getUpcomingSundayServices with the new parameters.
- Action: Recompute highlighted service (respecting any serviceId URL parameter) and re-render list.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The core state for this page is derived from URL parameters, interface responses, and page-level selection:
1. URL parameters:
   - serviceId: Optional. If present on load or navigation, it indicates which SundayService should be highlighted.
   - Optional additional URL parameters (such as fromDate or maxCount) may be used to control the query scope for getUpcomingSundayServices; if present, they override internal defaults. The page does not rely on localStorage for any state persistence.

2. Initial load:
   - On page initialization, the page parses serviceId and any other recognized parameters from the URL.
   - fromDate for getUpcomingSundayServices is set to the current date/time unless overridden by a URL parameter.
   - maxCount is set from a configuration value or an optional URL parameter.
   - The page calls getUpcomingSundayServices(fromDate, maxCount).

3. After data fetch:
   - The response provides services (an array of SundayService) and next_upcoming_service_id.
   - The page searches services for an item whose id matches serviceId from the URL.
   - If such an item is found, selectedServiceId is set to that id and it becomes the highlighted service.
   - If the URL serviceId is missing or not found in services, selectedServiceId is set to next_upcoming_service_id when that id is present in services.
   - If neither a matching serviceId nor a matching next_upcoming_service_id is found, selectedServiceId is left undefined, and the page may optionally default to the first item in services while not adding or modifying serviceId in the URL.

4. Ongoing interactions:
   - When a user selects a service from the list, selectedServiceId is updated and the URL serviceId parameter is updated to match (without adding additional persistent storage).
   - When a user uses the Clear Selection/Reset control, selectedServiceId is set back to next_upcoming_service_id (if present) and serviceId is removed from the URL.
   - When range controls change fromDate or maxCount, the page updates those values in the URL, refetches services via getUpcomingSundayServices, and recomputes selectedServiceId using the same precedence rules.

5. Derived state:
   - For each SundayService, the page can compute derived values such as remainingCapacity = capacity - reserved_seats (only when both fields are defined) and a boolean isReservable (true when status and capacity allow additional reservations). These values are computed at render time from the SundayService fields and are not stored in any external storage.
   - The highlighted service object is derived dynamically as services.find(s => s.id === selectedServiceId) each time state changes.

components.functionality: Fetches upcoming SundayService records from getUpcomingSundayServices based on current fromDate and maxCount state, and exposes the services array, next_upcoming_service_id, loading state, and error state to other components.

Displays the currently highlighted SundayService, showing its key details and a primary Reserve Seats action when isReservable is true, as well as a Reset to Next Upcoming control to clear any manual selection.

Renders the list of upcoming SundayService items returned by services-data-loader, sorted by start_datetime, and allows the user to select a service row to highlight it or click Reserve Seats for that specific item.
Given a single SundayService, computes and displays a status/availability label and determines whether the service is reservable based on fields such as status, capacity, and reserved_seats.
Provides optional controls to adjust the range of upcoming services shown (e.g., changing fromDate or maxCount) and triggers a refetch via services-data-loader when changed.
Offers an optional client-side filter to show only services where childcare_available is true, without making additional interface calls, and updates which services are visible in services-list.
Listens to loading and error states from services-data-loader and conditionally displays loading indicators, error messages, and a Retry action without altering the underlying data.

Synchronizes selectedServiceId, fromDate, and maxCount between internal page state and the URL parameters (serviceId and any optional date/range parameters) without using localStorage, ensuring deep links and back/forward navigation behave predictably.

Handles navigation to service_reservation.html when Reserve Seats is triggered from either highlighted-service-panel or services-list, constructing the target URL with the correct serviceId parameter.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single central column likely mapped to a standard 12-column grid, with all visible content centered within a full-width hero section.
layout.layout_pattern: All Sunday Services content sits within a single, vertically stacked central column over a full-width, photographic hero background (sunset / warm tones). From top to bottom: global navigation (outside this spec), then page heading (contextual), followed by the highlighted-service-panel occupying the visual center of the hero, then a compact band of filter controls (services-range-controls and services-childcare-toggle) directly beneath it, and finally the services-list extending downward as the user scrolls. No sidebars or multi-column layouts; everything is centered and aligned vertically to preserve the devotional, hero-based composition.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All Sunday Services content sits within a single, vertically stacked central column over a full-width, photographic hero background (sunset / warm tones). From top to bottom: global navigation (outside this spec), then page heading (contextual), followed by the highlighted-service-panel occupying the visual center of the hero, then a compact band of filter controls (services-range-controls and services-childcare-toggle) directly beneath it, and finally the services-list extending downward as the user scrolls. No sidebars or multi-column layouts; everything is centered and aligned vertically to preserve the devotional, hero-based composition.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Roughly the upper 20–25% of the viewport is reserved for navigation and the page title/context (not in this component list). The highlighted-service-panel lives in the central 35–45% of the initial viewport height, visually anchored where the background image is darkest for maximum contrast. Directly below, a shallow 10–15% band holds the filter controls. The remaining area is used for the services-list, which can extend beyond the first viewport and scroll vertically. Generous horizontal margins keep the central content to about 60–70% of the viewport width, maxing out around a typical content width (e.g., 960–1120px) while the background image spans edge-to-edge.
layout_strategies.content_density: The layout is intentionally sparse: one primary hero-style panel (highlighted-service-panel), a small cluster of filters, and a gently spaced list. Each block has generous vertical spacing (32–64px between related pieces, 64–96px between major sections) and limited text and controls per line. The services-list uses card-like rows with ample padding and whitespace between items rather than a dense table, keeping the page contemplative and airy.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye enters at the top center (nav and page title), then is pulled immediately to the highlighted-service-panel in the middle of the hero, which functions like the hero scripture in the mockup. Within that panel, attention moves from service title and date to the service-status-indicator and the primary call to action (reserve). From there, the eye naturally drops to the filter band (services-range-controls first, then the services-childcare-toggle), and finally flows into the vertically stacked services-list. On error or loading, the central column still acts as the primary focus, with messaging or spinners centered where the highlighted panel normally sits so the visual hierarchy and flow remain intact.

layout.overall_description: The Sunday Services page sits atop a full-bleed, warm-toned photographic background that fills the entire browser width and at least the first viewport height. A single, centered content column floats over this image, giving the sense of a devotional card placed on a photograph. At the heart of the screen, roughly mid-viewport, a large, softly elevated highlighted-service-panel acts as the focal hero, containing the next Sunday service details and a clear reserve action. Above it, the global nav and page title occupy a slender top band with ample breathing room. Beneath the hero panel, a thin, refined strip of filters (date range and childcare toggle) spans a modest width and is centered, echoing the stacked, centered scripture from the mockup. As users scroll downward, the central column continues seamlessly into the services-list: a vertical series of service cards, each with generous padding and spacing, framed by the same full-width background. Non-visual utility components operate behind the scenes, but all interactive and informational elements adhere to the single-column, centered, low-density design, preserving the reverent, meditative feel of the original hero layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: This is a non-visual infrastructure component instantiated at the top of the Sunday Services main content area, conceptually sitting behind the central column. It does not render any visible UI or occupy layout space; instead, it governs the data that populates the highlighted-service-panel and services-list. During loading, it triggers a page-level loading treatment: the hero background remains visible, and a subtle, centered spinner or loading indicator (styled consistent with the site’s minimal aesthetic) appears mid-viewport where the highlighted-service-panel would otherwise be, temporarily taking over that central space until data is ready.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible, non-layout element that influences the visibility of the central-column content; when in a loading state, it causes a centered loading indicator to occupy the same vertical position as the highlighted-service-panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The highlighted-service-panel is the primary hero card, positioned in the vertical center of the initial viewport, layered above the darker mid-region of the full-width background image for maximum legibility. It is horizontally centered and spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width, constrained to a comfortable max width (about 960px) and framed by generous left/right margins. The panel has substantial internal padding (24–32px on all sides) and a soft, possibly translucent background so that hints of the photograph may subtly show through while keeping text clear. Vertically, it sits about 20–25% down from the top of the viewport (below the navigation and page title band), taking up about 35–45% of the viewport height depending on content. Within the panel, the service title and time are center-aligned near the top, with the service-status-indicator directly beneath or just above them, and the primary reserve call-to-action located near the bottom center of the panel. The panel is visually separated from the filters and services-list below by a comfortable vertical gap of 48–64px.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the central column; it does not scroll independently but moves with the page. On smaller screens, it maintains its centered alignment and reduces to about 90% of viewport width while preserving generous vertical padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services-list begins just below the filter band, aligned to the same central column as the highlighted-service-panel. It is centered horizontally and spans a slightly narrower or equal width to the highlighted panel (about 60–70% of viewport width, max 960px), creating a continuous vertical column of content. Each service in the list appears as a card-like row with ample internal padding (16–24px) and a vertical gap of 24–32px between items, maintaining the low-density, meditative feel. The top edge of the first list item is separated from the filters above by 32–48px of whitespace, creating a clear but gentle transition from hero-focus to browsing. As the user scrolls, this list extends downward, with the background image either continuing or subtly shifting to a more neutral background while the central column alignment and spacing remain consistent.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, flowing vertically beneath the hero region. It scrolls naturally with the page and expands as more services are present, always maintaining centered alignment and consistent max width.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-status-indicator is rendered as a small, pill-shaped label or concise text block inside the highlighted-service-panel, positioned just below the service title and time, center-aligned. It occupies only as much width as its content requires (typically 10–25% of the panel width) and is surrounded by comfortable padding (8–16px horizontal, 4–8px vertical within the pill) with at least 8–12px of whitespace separating it from neighboring text. This placement keeps it clearly associated with the highlighted service while not overpowering the title or call-to-action. On narrower viewports, the status pill remains centered and flows naturally between the title and the main body content, preserving vertical stacking and generous spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, contained entirely within the highlighted-service-panel. It does not alter panel size significantly, but its presence might slightly increase the panel height while maintaining all around padding.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services-range-controls form the primary filter band directly beneath the highlighted-service-panel. They sit in a horizontal row (where space allows) centered within the same central column and spanning roughly 40–60% of the viewport width, never exceeding the width of the highlighted-service-panel. There is a vertical gap of about 48–64px between the bottom of the highlighted-service-panel and the top of these controls, echoing the ample spacing between hero text blocks in the mockup. The controls themselves (e.g., date selector or range buttons) are laid out in a single row with 16–24px horizontal spacing between individual elements, and 12–16px internal padding per control, maintaining a light visual footprint. On smaller screens, the range controls gracefully wrap into a stacked vertical arrangement (each control full-width up to the column) while retaining centered alignment and 8–16px vertical gaps between them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, positioned immediately below the highlighted-service-panel. It adjusts responsively from a centered horizontal cluster to a stacked vertical layout without breaking the single-column, centered composition.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services-childcare-toggle is visually grouped with the services-range-controls as part of the same filter band. On larger viewports, it appears to the right of or immediately below the main range control elements, still within the same centered width (40–60% of the viewport) and aligned to the same vertical baseline. It is slightly smaller and visually lighter than the range controls, with modest padding (8–12px horizontal, 4–8px vertical) and a gap of 16px from the nearest range control item, ensuring it feels related but subordinate. On narrow screens, it stacks beneath the range controls within the filter band, maintaining 8–12px vertical spacing and center alignment. The entire filter grouping sits as a single, low-profile band between the highlighted-service-panel above and the services-list below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, tightly coupled spatially to the services-range-controls. It flows with them as they reflow from horizontal to vertical arrangements, always remaining within the same centered filter band.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The services-loading-error-state manifests as a centered message block when data cannot be loaded, replacing the highlighted-service-panel and services-list content in the user’s view. When active, it is positioned in the same vertical space where the highlighted-service-panel normally appears: centered horizontally, roughly 20–25% down from the top of the viewport. The error block spans about 40–60% of the viewport width (max ~720px), with generous padding (24–32px) and ample vertical margins above and below (at least 48px) so it feels like a calm, self-contained message over the hero background rather than a jarring alert. The text is center-aligned, with a possible retry button placed directly beneath the message, mirroring the stacked, centered composition of the mockup’s scripture area. When this state is active, the filter band and services-list are hidden or visually de-emphasized to keep focus on the error block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Conditional, replacing the central-column content when an error occurs. It is static within the page flow but effectively overlays the normal hero content by hiding or withholding it while the error persists.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The url-state-synchronizer is a non-visual component that does not render any UI or occupy screen space. Conceptually, it is attached to the Sunday Services page container and mirrors the user’s current selection (highlighted service, filters) into the URL. Its presence does not change padding, margins, or the spatial arrangement of any visible elements; it simply ensures that the state of the highlighted-service-panel and services-list is encoded in the URL behind the scenes.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible and non-spatial; it has no direct layout behavior and operates entirely in the background.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reserve-navigation-handler is also non-visual, connected to interactive elements (like a 'Reserve Seat' button) inside the highlighted-service-panel and potentially within each item in the services-list. It does not occupy its own space; instead, it is conceptually bound to those controls, triggering navigation when they are used. From a layout perspective, all visible cues (buttons, links) remain within their respective panels and cards, centered and spaced according to the hero-style aesthetic, while this handler silently manages the transition to the reservation flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Invisible and non-spatial; it is logically anchored to buttons and links but does not create or modify any physical layout regions on the page.

name: Service Reservation
title: Service Reservation
description: Allows users to reserve seats for a specific Sunday service by selecting attendee counts, seating preferences, and providing contact information, then displays a reservation confirmation.

core_features: Load and display details for the selected Sunday service using the serviceId URL parameter and getSundayServiceDetail.
Show remaining seats and prevent users from requesting more seats than available based on seats_remaining from getSundayServiceDetail.
Provide a reservation form to collect number of adults, number of children, and individual child ages.
Allow users to choose a seating preference mapped to the seating_preference enum values in ServiceReservation.
Collect reservation contact information, including full name, email, optional phone, and optional notes.
Submit a new reservation via createServiceReservation and handle success or failure responses.
Display an inline confirmation summary after successful reservation using the response from createServiceReservation.
Load and display an existing reservation confirmation using reservationId in the URL and getServiceReservationConfirmation (e.g., from a confirmation email link).
Allow navigation back to the Sunday services listing (sunday_services.html) with the relevant serviceId preserved in the URL.
Allow navigation back to the website home page (index.html) with highlight=services to focus on service-related content.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Make a new reservation for a Sunday service
1. User arrives on service_reservation.html with a serviceId URL parameter (e.g., service_reservation.html?serviceId={ID}).
2. On page load, the page-state-router detects serviceId without reservationId and calls getSundayServiceDetail(sundayServiceId = serviceId).
3. service-detail-loader receives the SundayService object and seats_remaining, and displays a service summary (title, date, time, location, childcare availability, and seats remaining).
4. reservation-form becomes active and pre-binds to the loaded SundayService. Seats_remaining is used to validate attendee counts.
5. User enters num_adults and num_children. When num_children changes, children-ages-input-list updates to show one age input field per child.
6. User chooses a seating_preference option (front_section, middle_section, back_section, balcony, or no_preference) via seating-preference-selector.
7. User fills in contact_full_name, contact_email, optional contact_phone, and optional notes in contact-info-section.
8. On form submission, reservation-form validates required fields, ensures total attendees (num_adults + num_children) is greater than zero and does not exceed seats_remaining, and then calls createServiceReservation with collected values and the SundayService ID.
9. If createServiceReservation returns success = false, reservation-form displays the returned message as an error and leaves the form editable.
10. If createServiceReservation returns success = true, reservation-form handles the response: it reads reservation_id, status, message, and summary and updates the browser URL to include reservationId (e.g., service_reservation.html?serviceId={ID}&reservationId={reservation_id}) without reloading the page if possible.
11. reservation-confirmation-panel is shown in-place, using the summary object from the createServiceReservation response to display key confirmation information (service title, date/time, attendee counts, seating preference, and contact details).
12. User may optionally click 'Back to Services' to navigate to sunday_services.html?serviceId={current serviceId} or 'Return to Home' to go to index.html?highlight=services.

Workflow 2: View an existing reservation confirmation (e.g., from email)
1. User opens a link to service_reservation.html with a reservationId URL parameter (e.g., service_reservation.html?reservationId={RES_ID}). serviceId may or may not be present.
2. On page load, page-state-router detects reservationId and calls getServiceReservationConfirmation(reservationId).
3. getServiceReservationConfirmation returns a reservation (ServiceReservation) and its related service (SundayService).
4. reservation-confirmation-panel displays a confirmation view using the returned reservation and service: service title, date/time/location, num_adults, num_children, children_ages, seating_preference, contact_full_name, contact_email, and notes.
5. If the response indicates a valid ServiceReservation, page-state-router may optionally hide the reservation-form or show it in a read-only state, since the primary goal is to view confirmation.
6. If service.id is present, 'Back to Services' navigates to sunday_services.html?serviceId={service.id}; if not, it navigates to sunday_services.html without a serviceId parameter.
7. 'Return to Home' always navigates to index.html?highlight=services.

Workflow 3: Error handling and edge cases
1. User visits service_reservation.html without serviceId and without reservationId.
2. page-state-router detects missing identifiers and shows an error state in reservation-form or a dedicated message, instructing the user to select a service from the Sunday services listing.
3. A button or link is provided to navigate to sunday_services.html without parameters.
4. If getSundayServiceDetail fails (e.g., invalid or cancelled service) or returns a service whose status is not suitable for reservation (e.g., cancelled or completed), reservation-form is disabled and an informational message is displayed, possibly with a link back to sunday_services.html.
5. If seats_remaining is zero or less, reservation-form informs the user that the service is full and disables attendee inputs and submission, but still allows navigation via 'Back to Services'.
6. If getServiceReservationConfirmation fails or returns no matching reservation, reservation-confirmation-panel shows an appropriate message and may hide sensitive reservation details.
interactions: On page load, the page routes based on URL parameters (serviceId and reservationId) and triggers appropriate data-loading interfaces.
User clicks numeric input controls (or types) to set num_adults and num_children; changes immediately update internal state and validation messages.
User selects seating preference from a set of radio buttons or a dropdown; selection is mapped directly to the ServiceReservation.seating_preference enum values.
User fills in text inputs for contact_full_name, contact_email, contact_phone, and notes; validation triggers on blur and on submit.
When num_children changes, the children-ages-input-list dynamically shows or hides child age inputs to match the specified count, maintaining an ordered array of ages.
User submits the reservation-form; an onSubmit handler validates inputs, calls createServiceReservation, and then updates view state based on success or failure.

After a successful reservation, the confirmation view is displayed inline; the URL is updated to include reservationId (using history API or a full reload) to support re-loading the confirmation later.
User clicks 'Back to Services'; onClick constructs the URL sunday_services.html with the current serviceId (if available) and navigates there.
User clicks 'Return to Home'; onClick navigates to index.html with highlight=services.
User may refresh the page while viewing a confirmation with reservationId; onLoad logic detects reservationId and uses getServiceReservationConfirmation to rehydrate the confirmation state.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is fully driven by URL parameters and interface responses:

1. URL parameters
- serviceId: Identifies the SundayService being reserved. Used to load service details (getSundayServiceDetail) and to maintain context when navigating back to sunday_services.html.
- reservationId: Identifies an existing ServiceReservation for confirmation. Used to load reservation and service details (getServiceReservationConfirmation).

2. Initial routing rules (on page load)
- If reservationId is present (regardless of serviceId):
  - Call getServiceReservationConfirmation(reservationId).
  - If a valid reservation and service are returned, display reservation-confirmation-panel with those details.
  - Optionally use reservation.sunday_service_id from the returned reservation to infer serviceId for navigation. If needed, the page can update the URL to include serviceId without reloading.
  - The reservation-form is hidden or shown in a read-only state, depending on the desired UX, but no new reservation submission occurs automatically.

- Else if serviceId is present and reservationId is not present:
  - Call getSundayServiceDetail(sundayServiceId = serviceId).
  - If successful and the service is reservable (status indicates scheduled and seats_remaining > 0 according to business rules implemented in the UI), display service summary and enable the reservation-form.
  - If the service cannot be reserved (e.g., cancelled, completed, or seats_remaining <= 0), display the service summary and a non-interactive notice instead of an active submission form.

- Else (no serviceId and no reservationId):
  - Show an error/instructional state indicating that no service was selected.
  - Provide navigation to sunday_services.html so the user can choose a service.

3. Post-submission state changes
- On successful createServiceReservation call, the page:
  - Reads reservation_id from the response.
  - Updates the browser URL to include reservationId (e.g., by pushing a new history state: service_reservation.html?serviceId={current serviceId}&reservationId={reservation_id}).
  - Switches to confirmation state, showing reservation-confirmation-panel populated from the summary object in the response.
  - The reservation-form may become hidden or locked to avoid duplicate submissions.

- On failed createServiceReservation call (success = false):
  - The page leaves the URL unchanged and keeps reservationId absent.
  - Error details from message are displayed above or within the reservation-form.

4. Refresh and direct access behavior
- When the page is reloaded with reservationId present, confirmation data is always reloaded via getServiceReservationConfirmation, not via cached client state.
- When the page is reloaded with only serviceId present, service details and seats_remaining are always loaded via getSundayServiceDetail.

5. Navigation links
- 'Back to Services':
