  - If a known serviceId is available from the current URL or from the loaded service/reservation, navigate to sunday_services.html?serviceId={that serviceId}.
  - If no serviceId is available, navigate to sunday_services.html without parameters.

- 'Return to Home':
  - Navigate to index.html?highlight=services to indicate that the services section should be emphasized on the home page.

No localStorage or client-side persistence outside of the URL is used to manage page state; all durable state is re-derived from the URL parameters and the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Evaluates URL parameters (serviceId, reservationId) on load and decides whether to load service details for a new reservation or reservation confirmation details for an existing reservation. Controls the visibility and mode of the reservation-form and reservation-confirmation-panel.

Calls getSundayServiceDetail with the sundayServiceId derived from the serviceId URL parameter (or from a reservation's sunday_service_id) and provides the returned SundayService and seats_remaining to downstream components.

Displays a non-editable summary of the selected Sunday service, including title, date and time, location details, childcare availability, service status, and seats_remaining. Indicates whether reservations are still available based on seats_remaining and service status.

Collects all inputs needed to create a ServiceReservation for the loaded SundayService: num_adults, num_children, children_ages, seating_preference, contact_full_name, contact_email, contact_phone, and notes. Validates inputs against seats_remaining and required fields, then calls createServiceReservation on submit.

Dynamically renders and manages a list of child age inputs based on the current num_children value in the reservation-form. Maintains children_ages as an ordered array of numbers that is passed to createServiceReservation.

Provides a set of mutually exclusive seating preference options mapped directly to the seating_preference enum values (front_section, middle_section, back_section, balcony, no_preference) and updates the reservation-form state accordingly.

Captures contact_full_name (required), contact_email (required), contact_phone (optional), and notes (optional) for the reservation. Performs field-level validation (e.g., email format) and shares values with reservation-form for submission.

When a reservationId is present in the URL, calls getServiceReservationConfirmation(reservationId) and provides the returned reservation and service data to reservation-confirmation-panel and page-state-router.

Displays confirmation details for a reservation using either the summary from a recent createServiceReservation response or the data returned from getServiceReservationConfirmation. Shows service summary, attendee counts, seating preference, contact details, and reservation status.
Displays success, information, and error messages related to loading services, loading confirmations, validation failures, and createServiceReservation responses.

Provides contextual navigation actions: 'Back to Services' (navigates to sunday_services.html with optional serviceId parameter) and 'Return to Home' (navigates to index.html with highlight=services). Uses the latest known serviceId from URL or loaded data.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single central column likely mapped to a standard 12-column grid, with all visible content centered within a full-width hero section.
layout.layout_pattern: All visible components are arranged in a single, centered vertical column over a full-bleed photographic hero background. Navigation sits at the very top, followed by any global message banner, then a succinct service summary and page title, then the reservation form or confirmation panel stacked below. Form subcomponents are vertically stacked within the main form, maintaining a clear top-to-bottom reading order. Data and state components exist logically around this column but have no visual footprint.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All visible components are arranged in a single, centered vertical column over a full-bleed photographic hero background. Navigation sits at the very top, followed by any global message banner, then a succinct service summary and page title, then the reservation form or confirmation panel stacked below. Form subcomponents are vertically stacked within the main form, maintaining a clear top-to-bottom reading order. Data and state components exist logically around this column but have no visual footprint.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top 20–25% of the viewport is reserved for navigation, message banner (when present), and the service summary heading. The central 45–55% of the viewport is dedicated to the primary interactive card (reservation form or confirmation panel), centered over the darker portion of the background image for legibility. The remaining lower portion of the viewport remains largely image-only, with generous breathing room around the central column. All textual content is constrained to a central column around 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, with 16–32px internal padding on cards and 64–96px vertical spacing between major sections.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low, mirroring the devotional hero feel: a single main column, few concurrent blocks, and ample whitespace between sections. Within the reservation form, fields are grouped into gentle clusters with 16–24px vertical spacing, ensuring the form still feels calm and unhurried rather than dense or transactional.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is led from the top navigation, down past any message banner to the service summary (acting as the page’s main heading), then naturally into the reservation form. Strong vertical alignment and centered layout help maintain focus. The background image gradient is darker behind the central card to guide attention to the form or confirmation copy, while the lighter upper and lower areas keep the overall composition serene and open.

layout.overall_description: The Service Reservation page appears as a full-bleed hero section with a warm, sunset-toned photograph filling the entire viewport. At the very top, a thin horizontal navigation bar with the ministry name in an elegant serif and simple navigation links stretches full width but keeps its content centered. Directly beneath, a subtle, full-width message banner may appear as a translucent strip if there is a status message, gently pushing the content below it. Anchored in the upper-middle of the hero, a centered service summary panel acts as the page heading, briefly stating the specific Sunday service details in a reverent tone. Below this, occupying the central band of the screen, a softly elevated card holds either the reservation form or the reservation confirmation, depending on state. This card is centered horizontally, with generous internal padding and stacked fields that leave breathing room between each group. The children’s ages fields, seating preference selector, and contact information sit as clearly separated sections within this card, following the same vertical rhythm. The edges of the screen, especially the lower third, remain mostly free of text, allowing the background image and subtle gradient to frame the central content and preserve the spacious, contemplative atmosphere of the original hero design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page-state-router has no visible UI; it conceptually sits behind the entire hero layout, controlling whether the central interactive area shows the reservation form or the reservation confirmation panel. From a spatial perspective, it governs which main card occupies the central 45–55% of the viewport height, directly beneath the service summary panel, but does not occupy its own space in the visual column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, logical layer that swaps primary content within the central column without altering the surrounding layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-detail-loader runs invisibly before or as the hero is displayed, fetching details for the service summary and form. It occupies no visible space but functionally feeds the service-summary-panel and reservation-form that sit in the upper-middle and middle of the hero, respectively.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual data layer; operates full-screen but does not affect layout dimensions.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The service-summary-panel is placed near the upper-middle of the hero, centered horizontally and aligned within the single central column. It sits approximately 15–20% down from the top edge of the viewport, just below the navigation bar and any message banner. The panel spans about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, with 24–32px internal padding and a translucent or subtly elevated background for legibility over the image. Its text (service name, date, time) is center-aligned in a typography that echoes the brand’s serif heading style, acting as the main page title. There is roughly 24–32px of vertical space below this panel before the reservation-form or confirmation card begins, maintaining the airy, devotional spacing.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows inline within the central column; position is vertically fixed relative to surrounding content but scrolls with the page if content extends beyond one viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reservation-form appears as the main interactive card centered in the middle of the hero, directly below the service-summary-panel. It spans about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop and is vertically sized to fit its content, typically occupying the central 40–50% of the viewport height, with at least 64px of breathing room above and below before reaching the top navigation area or the quieter lower part of the image. The form sits on a soft, slightly opaque panel with rounded corners and 24–32px padding on all sides to preserve the reverent, open feel. All fields are stacked vertically within a single column, center-aligned as a block, with 16–24px spacing between groups of fields. Subsections (children-ages-input-list, seating-preference-selector, contact-info-section) appear as distinct vertical groups within this card, each separated by increased vertical spacing (24–32px) and optional subtle dividers, yet all visually unified as part of one calm, continuous card.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Central, inline card in the main column; scrollable with the page if the form height exceeds the viewport, but generally designed to fit within one screen on desktop where possible.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The children-ages-input-list is a subsection within the reservation-form card, positioned after the general attendee count fields and before seating preferences. It occupies the full internal width of the form card (minus the 24–32px padding) and is aligned with the form’s single column. A small heading or label is centered or left-aligned within the card’s column, with 8–16px of space above the first child age input. Each child age row is stacked vertically with 8–12px spacing between rows to prevent crowding. The overall block takes up roughly 15–25% of the form card’s height, depending on the number of entries, and has 24px of vertical margin separating it from the sections above and below, preserving the gentle rhythm of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows as an inline block within the reservation form; its height expands downward as more child age inputs are added, pushing subsequent sections further down while keeping the column centered.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The seating-preference-selector appears as the next major section within the reservation-form, directly below the children-ages-input-list (or below the general attendee section if there are no children). It occupies the full width of the form card’s inner column and is vertically separated from the previous section by 24–32px. The selector options (e.g., near front, middle, back, accessible seating) are arranged in a vertical list or a row of pill-like buttons that still respect the single-column, centered layout. The section is compact, taking up around 10–15% of the form card’s height, with center-aligned labels to maintain harmony with the rest of the form.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the form’s vertical flow; does not change overall layout behavior but can slightly adjust in height depending on number of options.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The contact-info-section forms the final major block within the reservation-form, positioned near the lower portion of the card after seating preferences. It spans the full width of the form card’s content area, following the same single-column structure. Individual fields (name, email, phone, etc.) are stacked vertically with 12–16px spacing, and the entire block typically occupies 20–30% of the form card’s height. A clear section label sits above the first field with 8–12px of spacing, and there is approximately 24–32px of space below the last contact field before any submission controls (e.g., Reserve Seats button). This placement reinforces its importance while keeping the overall density calm and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline within the form; its height is content-driven but designed to remain within the central hero card, preserving margins above and below.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reservation-confirmation-loader operates invisibly when a reservation is submitted, momentarily taking control of the central card content. It does not add any new visible area itself but may trigger a lightweight loading state within the same central region previously occupied by the reservation-form, keeping the user’s focus anchored in the center of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual data layer; temporarily overlays or replaces content inside the central card area without changing the global page layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reservation-confirmation-panel occupies the same central card footprint as the reservation-form, centered horizontally and vertically in the middle band of the hero. Once the reservation is complete, this panel replaces the form within the central column, spanning 60–70% of the viewport width with 24–32px padding. Its height is typically slightly less than the form’s, sitting comfortably in the central 35–45% of the viewport height and leaving generous open image space above and below. The confirmation text is center-aligned, with a prominent confirmation heading near the top of the card, key reservation details in the middle, and any follow-up instructions or actions near the bottom. Vertical spacing between text blocks follows the 16–24px rhythm, preserving the serene, reverent tone of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Central inline card that swaps into the main column in place of the reservation form; scrolls with the page if content height grows but generally fits within a single viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The message-banner is a slim, full-width horizontal strip positioned directly below the navigation bar at the very top of the page. It stretches edge-to-edge across the viewport but keeps its message text centered within the same central column width as the main content. The banner height is modest—roughly 40–56px depending on content—and it uses a semi-translucent overlay so the hero image subtly shows through. When present, it pushes the service-summary-panel slightly lower, maintaining at least 24–32px of vertical space between the bottom of the banner and the top of the summary panel. When not present, the service-summary-panel shifts upward, reclaiming the space and preserving the open top area.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline at the top of the layout under navigation; appears and disappears dynamically, shifting the column below it but not overlapping other content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The navigation-links component sits in a full-width horizontal bar at the very top of the page, overlaying the hero background. The bar itself is visually light—either transparent or with a subtle gradient—so the background image remains visible while the links are legible. Within this bar, content is centered within the same maximum width as the main column. The ministry name or logo, set in an elegant serif, appears near the center or left-center, with navigation links arranged either side-by-side in a single row. The bar occupies roughly the top 10–12% of the viewport height, leaving a comfortable gap (24–32px) between its lower edge and the service-summary-panel or message-banner. Its presence frames the page but does not crowd the central hero content.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Top-of-page element that sits at the start of the scroll; it can be implemented as static (scrolling away with content) to mirror the original hero behavior, or as a subtle sticky bar if needed, but visually it is treated as the upper frame of the hero.

name: Bible Reading Plans
title: Bible Reading Plans
description: Manage your Bible reading plans: view progress, open plan details, adjust reminders, pause, delete, or create new plans.

core_features: Load and display the current user's Bible reading plans using getBibleReadingPlansList, showing key details such as name, status, duration, and progress_percent.
Allow users to open a specific plan to see its detailed schedule (list of ReadingPlanDay items) using getReadingPlanDetail.
Provide a clear action to create a new Bible reading plan by navigating to plan_builder.html with mode=create.
Allow editing an existing Bible reading plan by navigating to plan_builder.html with mode=edit and the selected planId.
Allow users to update daily reminder settings (daily_reminder_enabled and daily_reminder_time) for each plan via updateBibleReadingPlanNotifications.
Allow users to pause an active plan using pauseBibleReadingPlan and immediately reflect updated status in the UI.
Allow users to delete/cancel a plan using deleteBibleReadingPlan and remove it from the list once deletion succeeds.
Handle an optional focusPlanId URL parameter to automatically scroll to and expand a specific plan (such as one just created or edited on plan_builder.html) and load its details.
user_workflows: Viewing existing plans and progress: 1) User navigates to plans.html (via header link or direct URL). 2) Page controller calls getBibleReadingPlansList with no parameters. 3) When the response is returned, the plans-list component renders one list item per plan, showing fields such as name, status, duration_days, chapters_per_day, start_date, end_date, and progress_percent. 4) If no plans are returned, an empty-state component displays guidance to create a new plan.

Opening a plan and viewing its schedule: 1) From the list, the user clicks on a plan row or a dedicated 'View details' control. 2) The page sets this plan as the currently expanded plan in page state and updates the URL parameter focusPlanId to the selected plan_id (using history.replaceState or equivalent). 3) The plan-detail-panel component calls getReadingPlanDetail with planId equal to the selected plan_id. 4) When the response is returned, the component displays the full plan metadata and its days array (ReadingPlanDay records), including day_number, date (if present), passages, is_completed, and completed_at. 5) The expanded plan remains open until the user closes it or opens another plan.

Using focusPlanId from plan_builder.html: 1) User completes creating or editing a plan on plan_builder.html, which then redirects or links to plans.html with a URL parameter focusPlanId set to the relevant plan_id. 2) On load, the plans-page-controller reads focusPlanId from the URL. 3) After getBibleReadingPlansList completes, the controller checks if any plan_id matches the focusPlanId. 4) If a match is found, the controller marks that plan as expanded, scrolls it into view, and triggers getReadingPlanDetail for that plan. 5) The details display so the user can immediately see the schedule and settings of their newly created or edited plan.

Creating a new plan: 1) On plans.html, the user clicks the 'Create Plan' or 'New Plan' button. 2) The create-plan-button component triggers a navigation to plan_builder.html with URL parameters mode=create (e.g., plan_builder.html?mode=create). 3) The user completes the plan setup on plan_builder.html. 4) plan_builder.html, after saving, can send the user back to plans.html with focusPlanId equal to the new plan_id so that the new plan appears expanded when they return.

Editing an existing plan: 1) For a specific plan in the list, the user clicks an 'Edit' action within the plan-actions-menu component. 2) The page navigates to plan_builder.html with mode=edit and planId equal to that plan's plan_id (e.g., plan_builder.html?mode=edit&planId={plan_id}). 3) The user updates the plan in the builder and saves. 4) plan_builder.html can return the user to plans.html?focusPlanId={plan_id} so that the updated plan is expanded and in view.

Updating notification settings: 1) Within the expanded plan detail, the user interacts with notification controls (for example, a toggle for daily_reminder_enabled and a time picker for daily_reminder_time). 2) When the user confirms changes (e.g., clicks 'Save reminders'), the notification-settings-form component calls updateBibleReadingPlanNotifications with planId, dailyReminderEnabled, and dailyReminderTime derived from the form inputs. 3) When the API responds, the component updates the displayed reminder status and time based on the returned plan object. 4) The plans-list is also updated for this plan to reflect the new reminder state so that list and detail remain consistent.

Pausing a plan: 1) For an active plan, the user clicks a 'Pause plan' action within the plan-actions-menu component. 2) The component shows a confirmation prompt; on confirmation, it calls pauseBibleReadingPlan with the planId. 3) When the API returns success and the updated plan object, the component updates the corresponding list item and detail panel status to the new status (for example, from active to paused). 4) Any UI elements that depend on plan status (such as action labels or whether a pause button is shown) update in real time.

Deleting a plan: 1) For any plan, the user clicks a 'Delete plan' or 'Cancel plan' action in the plan-actions-menu. 2) A confirmation dialog appears explaining that the plan and its schedule will be removed. 3) Upon confirmation, the component calls deleteBibleReadingPlan with the planId. 4) If the API returns success=true, the page removes that plan from the in-memory plans list and re-renders the plans-list without that entry (or optionally re-calls getBibleReadingPlansList to refresh). 5) If the deleted plan was currently expanded or referenced by focusPlanId, the controller clears the expanded selection and updates the URL to remove focusPlanId.

Adjusting which plan is in focus via URL: 1) A user receives or saves a URL to plans.html that includes a focusPlanId value. 2) When visiting that URL, plans.html loads the list and automatically highlights and expands that specific plan (if it exists) by invoking getReadingPlanDetail. 3) If the user then selects a different plan, the URL is updated to the new plan_id in focusPlanId so that browser navigation and sharing preserve the newly selected plan.
interactions: On page load, the plans-page-controller automatically calls getBibleReadingPlansList. While loading, the plans-list component can show a generic loading state; once the response arrives, each plan is rendered as a clickable list item.

Clicking on a plan list item (or an explicit 'View details' link) toggles that plan's expanded state. If expanding, the plan-detail-panel triggers getReadingPlanDetail with the selected planId to load the plan and its days; if collapsing, the details are hidden without an API call.
The 'Create Plan' button is clickable and triggers navigation to plan_builder.html with mode=create in the query string, without calling any data interfaces from this page.

Each plan item includes an 'Edit' interaction that, when clicked, navigates the browser to plan_builder.html with mode=edit and planId equal to that plan's plan_id, preserving the user's location through URL parameters.

Within the expanded plan detail, the user can toggle a control for daily_reminder_enabled. Changing this value enables or disables the time selection input in the notification-settings-form component before submitting changes.

When the user clicks 'Save reminders' in the notification-settings-form, the component validates that when dailyReminderEnabled is true, a dailyReminderTime value is present and in a valid time-of-day format. It then calls updateBibleReadingPlanNotifications; UI elements are disabled during the request and re-enabled when the response arrives.

If updateBibleReadingPlanNotifications returns success=false or an error, the notification-settings-form presents an error message and reverts the toggle/time values to the last known successful state from the API.

Clicking the 'Pause plan' action invokes a confirmation UI; confirming sends pauseBibleReadingPlan with the planId. While the request is in progress, the action is disabled and a loading indicator is shown. On success, the plan's status display updates, and any UI that depends on status (for example, showing a 'Resume' label instead of 'Pause') updates accordingly, even though resuming is not handled by an interface on this page.

Clicking the 'Delete plan' action triggers a confirmation dialog; confirming calls deleteBibleReadingPlan with the planId. While processing, the delete action is disabled. Once success is returned, the plan is removed from the list and, if it was expanded, the detail view is hidden. If deletion fails, the plan remains visible and an error message is shown.

Clicking on different plans updates which plan is expanded and also updates the focusPlanId in the URL via history.replaceState (or similar), without full page reload. Using the browser's back/forward buttons restores expanded plan state based on the URL parameter.

If focusPlanId in the URL does not match any plan in the getBibleReadingPlansList response (for example, the plan was deleted), the controller ignores focusPlanId, does not auto-expand any plan, and optionally clears the parameter from the URL.
page_functionality.state_logic: The primary external state for this page is managed via URL parameters, particularly focusPlanId. On initial load, the plans-page-controller reads focusPlanId from the URL query string. After getBibleReadingPlansList returns, if a plan with plan_id equal to focusPlanId is found, that plan is marked as the "focused" and expanded plan; the controller scrolls to that plan and calls getReadingPlanDetail with that planId to populate the detail component. If no matching plan is found, no plan is auto-expanded, and the controller may remove the focusPlanId parameter from the URL to avoid confusion. When the user manually expands a plan by clicking on it, the controller sets that plan as the focused plan in in-memory state and updates the URL's focusPlanId parameter (using history.replaceState or equivalent) to the selected plan_id so that the URL always reflects the current focus without reloading the page. Collapsing a plan may clear focusPlanId from the URL, or leave it pointing to the last-focused plan, depending on the chosen UX; in either case, the mapping is handled via URL updates rather than localStorage. Navigation away from this page uses URL parameters as defined: clicking 'Create Plan' navigates to plan_builder.html?mode=create; clicking 'Edit' on a plan navigates to plan_builder.html?mode=edit&planId={plan_id}. When plan_builder.html sends users back to plans.html, it can include focusPlanId in the return URL (for example, plans.html?focusPlanId={plan_id}), which this page then uses as described. All other state, such as the current list of plans, the expanded/collapsed status of plans, and temporary form values for notification settings, is kept in memory on this page and synchronized with the server only through the assigned interfaces.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading, URL parameter handling, and high-level page state for Bible reading plans. On load, reads focusPlanId from the URL, calls getBibleReadingPlansList, manages which plan is expanded, and triggers getReadingPlanDetail when required. It updates the URL focusPlanId whenever the user changes the focused plan and ensures components receive up-to-date plan data.

Displays the list of Bible reading plans returned by getBibleReadingPlansList. Each item shows plan-level information such as name, content_scope, duration_days, chapters_per_day, start_date, end_date, status, progress_percent, daily_reminder_enabled, and daily_reminder_time. It allows selecting a plan to view details and delegates actions such as pause, delete, and edit to child components.

Represents a single Bible reading plan in the list. Renders key metadata and a compact progress view. Provides controls to expand/collapse details, navigate to edit, pause the plan, delete the plan, and open the notification settings. Communicates user actions back up to the controller or parent list.

Shows full detail for the currently selected Bible reading plan. When given a planId, it uses getReadingPlanDetail to load the plan object and its associated days array (ReadingPlanDay). It presents the schedule (e.g., day_number, date, passages, is_completed, completed_at) in a structured list and includes embedded notification settings and action controls for that specific plan.

Allows the user to view and update daily reminder settings for a specific plan. It initializes fields from the plan's daily_reminder_enabled and daily_reminder_time values. When the user submits changes, it calls updateBibleReadingPlanNotifications with planId, dailyReminderEnabled, and dailyReminderTime, then updates the plan data in both the detail panel and the list.

Provides plan-level actions such as editing, pausing, and deleting. For 'Edit', it triggers navigation to plan_builder.html with mode=edit and the planId. For 'Pause', it calls pauseBibleReadingPlan. For 'Delete', it calls deleteBibleReadingPlan. It also relays status changes back to the controller so that list and detail states stay synchronized.

A dedicated control for creating a new Bible reading plan. When activated, it navigates to plan_builder.html with mode=create in the query parameters, without invoking any of the data interfaces on this page.

Displays contextual guidance when getBibleReadingPlansList returns an empty plans array, prompting the user to create a new Bible reading plan via the create-plan-button. It is only shown when there are no plans to render.

Manages synchronization between the currently focused plan in the UI and the focusPlanId URL parameter. On initial load, it reads focusPlanId and requests that the controller focus that plan after the list is loaded. Whenever the user changes the focused plan, it updates the URL parameter without reloading the page.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single central column likely mapped to a standard 12-column grid, with all visible content centered within a full-width hero section.
layout.layout_pattern: Use a single, centered vertical column placed over a full-bleed background image, echoing the hero-style mockup. All functional components (page controller, create button, list, empty state, overlays) are stacked from top to bottom in one column, horizontally centered. No sidebars or multi-column splits; even when showing details, the content remains centered and vertical.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: Use a single, centered vertical column placed over a full-bleed background image, echoing the hero-style mockup. All functional components (page controller, create button, list, empty state, overlays) are stacked from top to bottom in one column, horizontally centered. No sidebars or multi-column splits; even when showing details, the content remains centered and vertical.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: Reserve roughly the top 20–25% of the viewport for the page-level controller (title, brief description) and the primary create-plan action. The central 40–50% of the viewport is dedicated to either the plans list or the empty state, visually anchored over the more contrasted middle of the background for legibility, similar to the scripture text placement. The remaining lower area is mostly open background, with the list or detail content naturally scrolling into it but maintaining generous top and bottom breathing space. Left and right edges maintain wide margins so the central column never exceeds about 70–80% of the viewport width.
layout_strategies.content_density: Maintain a low content density with a small number of clearly separated blocks: a distinct top header block, a clear action area, and a single content block (list or empty state) per screenful. Individual list items are generously padded and spaced (24–32px padding, 16–24px gaps) to keep a calm, devotional atmosphere. Detail views and forms are grouped into a few coherent sections rather than many small controls.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: Preserve a strong top-to-bottom visual flow: user attention starts at the centered page title (in a more expressive serif, echoing the brand in the mockup), moves down to the prominent create-plan button, then flows into the plans list or empty state in the central area. When a plan is opened, focus shifts to a centered modal-like detail panel that sits in the visual center of the screen, similar to how the scripture block is framed by the sunburst in the mockup. Background imagery and any top-to-bottom gradient should subtly guide the eye from the lighter top down into the more focused central content.

layout.overall_description: The Bible Reading Plans page sits over a full-bleed, warm-toned background image that echoes the hero from the mockup—think soft sunset gradients with darker tones toward the center where text will sit. A single, centered content column (max width around 960px) floats above a subtle top-to-bottom overlay gradient that darkens the middle third for legibility. At the top, occupying roughly the upper quarter of the viewport, a reverent serif page title and short description are centered, with a prominent create-plan button directly beneath, all spaced with generous 24–32px vertical gaps. Below this, in the visual center of the page, either a vertically stacked list of reading plans or a contemplative empty state block appears, aligned over the darker band of the background similar to the scripture placement in the mockup. Each plan card is centered in the column, padded and surrounded by whitespace so the edges of the screen are largely image-only, enhancing the devotional feel. When a plan is opened, the rest of the page subtly fades back and a softly elevated, centered detail panel appears in the middle 60–70% of the viewport, with the notification settings form stacked inside it, mirroring the original mockup’s focused scripture block. The entire experience feels like a single, tall hero section that transitions from contemplative header to focused engagement, maintaining a calm, spiritual tone throughout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plans-page-controller occupies the top center of the main content area, directly beneath the global site navigation (not defined here). It spans the full width of the central column (up to about 960px), horizontally centered in the viewport with wide left and right margins showing the background image. Vertically, it fills roughly the upper 20–25% of the viewport height. Inside this block, the page title (e.g., 'Bible Reading Plans') is set in an elegant serif, in uppercase or small caps, centered horizontally. It sits about 64px below the top edge of the content area, with at least 24–32px of whitespace above and below it. A brief descriptive line can sit beneath the title in a neutral sans-serif, with 8–16px spacing, maintaining a calm, airy feel. The bottom edge of this controller block is clearly separated from the next section (create button and list/empty state) by a vertical gap of about 32–48px, following the hero-like spaciousness.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Participates in normal document flow as the first block after any global header; it scrolls away with the page and does not stick or float.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plans-list is positioned immediately below the combined header area (plans-page-controller and create-plan-button), centered within the same main column. Its top edge starts roughly 48–64px beneath the create-plan-button to keep a clear, restful separation between the high-level actions and the detailed content. The list’s width is constrained to about 70–80% of the central column (roughly 640–720px), ensuring generous background margins on both sides; the list itself remains strictly one column of vertically stacked items. The visible portion of the list primarily occupies the central 40–50% of the viewport height; as more items are added, the page scrolls, allowing cards to move down into the lower, more open part of the hero while still leaving plenty of breathing space at the very bottom. Each plan-list-item sits within this list as an individual card, aligned center, with consistent 16–24px vertical gaps separating them.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard scrolling content within the main vertical flow; no fixed positioning. The list extends downward as needed, maintaining consistent top and bottom spacing relative to surrounding elements.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each plan-list-item appears as a single, centered card within the plans-list, visually resembling a devotional card laid over the background. The card width is about 70–80% of the central column, so that the card’s edges never get too close to the viewport edges; this leaves at least 10–15% of the viewport width as open background on each side. The card has generous padding—24–32px on all sides—creating a calm, airy interior. The plan title is placed at the top of the card, left-aligned internally but still centered as part of the card itself in the page context, using a serif or semi-serif style to echo the main title. Secondary details (progress, next reading, reminder summary) are stacked beneath in a sans-serif, with 8–16px vertical spacing between lines. The plan-actions-menu sits aligned to the right side of the card’s top row (inline with or just below the title), but stays within the padded card boundaries to keep outer edges clean. Vertically, each plan card has at least 16–24px of whitespace separating it from the next card, keeping the list visually light and uncluttered.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Flows within the plans-list as repeated blocks. On hover or focus, the card may subtly elevate or brighten but remains in place without changing layout or affecting neighboring cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-detail-panel appears as a centered, modal-like overlay above the hero background and the underlying list. When opened, it is fixed to the viewport, horizontally and vertically centered, occupying approximately 60–70% of the viewport width (capped around 720–800px) and 60–70% of the viewport height. This places the panel squarely in the visual center of the screen, mirroring how the scripture block was framed in the original mockup. The area outside the panel is dimmed with a soft, transparent dark overlay so the background image and cards remain faintly visible, reinforcing depth without distraction. Inside the panel, content is arranged in a single vertical column: a prominent plan title at the top, then progress and meta information, then the notification-settings-form. The panel uses generous 32px padding on all sides, and sections within are separated by 24–32px to maintain a devotional, uncluttered feel. The top of the panel remains at least 48px from the top of the viewport, ensuring the panel feels visually centered and not cramped against the browser edge.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed, modal overlay that sits above the normal document flow. It locks to the viewport center and typically prevents background scrolling while open, maintaining focus on the active plan.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The notification-settings-form is placed within the lower half of the plan-detail-panel, following any plan title and summary information. It occupies the full inner width of the panel’s content area but keeps its fields in a single vertical stack. Form controls (toggles, dropdowns, time pickers) are left-aligned within this column, with labels and inputs grouped in rows that span 100% of the panel content width up to about 600px; if the panel is wider, the form content stays centered within it to preserve balance. Vertical spacing between form rows is 16–24px, and groupings (such as basic reminder on/off vs. advanced schedule options) are separated by slightly larger 24–32px gaps. There is at least 24px of space above the first form element (following the previous section) and 32px of space below the final action buttons (e.g., Save, Cancel) to keep the form feeling light and not pressed against the edges of the panel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Contained entirely within the plan-detail-panel’s fixed overlay; it scrolls internally only if the panel’s vertical space is exceeded, otherwise it remains static within the panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan-actions-menu appears as a compact control (e.g., an icon button or subtle text button) located at the top right corner within each plan-list-item card. It aligns horizontally with the plan title’s baseline or sits just to its right, and is vertically centered within the top row of the card. The menu stays inside the card’s 24–32px padding, so it never touches the card’s outer edges, maintaining the clean, framed look. When expanded, its dropdown or action list opens downward, still anchored to the right side of the card’s interior; the dropdown should not extend beyond the card’s right edge but may overlay the card’s content beneath. Spacing around the menu is minimal but consistent (8–12px from surrounding text), ensuring that it feels accessible yet unobtrusive in the overall composition.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline element within each plan-list-item. Its collapsed state takes minimal horizontal space; its expanded state overlays locally within the card without pushing other layout elements out of position.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The create-plan-button is placed directly beneath the page title and description within the plans-page-controller area, forming the primary call-to-action in the upper portion of the page. It is centered horizontally within the main column and visually aligned with the page title above. Vertically, it sits about 24–32px below the descriptive text (or the title if no description is present), with at least 32–48px of whitespace underneath before the plans-list or empty-plans-state begins. The button has a generous tappable size—padding of 12–16px vertically and 24–32px horizontally—so it reads as a strong yet gentle accent akin to the bright cross in the hero mockup. The button’s width is intrinsic (based on its label), but on narrow viewports it should not exceed about 60% of the content width to avoid feeling edge-to-edge; it remains a focal point in the top 20–25% of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Inline block within the header area, participating in normal flow between the page title and the list/empty state. It scrolls with the page and does not stick to the bottom or top.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-plans-state replaces the plans-list when there are no plans. It is centered both horizontally and vertically within the content portion of the page, occupying roughly the central 40–50% of the viewport height. This state appears directly below the create-plan-button, with a generous 48–64px gap, so it feels like a natural continuation of the hero-style title area. The empty state content—a devotional illustration or icon, a short contemplative message, and a secondary call-to-action—forms a compact vertical stack with elements centered. The block width is limited to about 60–70% of the central column (roughly 480–640px), leaving surrounding background visible on all sides to maintain openness. Vertical spacing between the icon, message, and button is 16–24px, with at least 32px of space above the first element and below the last within the block, echoing the calm spacing around the scripture text in the original hero.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard block-level element that replaces the plans-list region when no data is present. It scrolls with the rest of the page but is usually fully visible without scrolling on common viewport heights.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The focus-plan-url-sync is a non-visual infrastructure component and therefore has no direct spatial footprint. Conceptually, it anchors to the page as a behavior that keeps the currently focused plan (and its detail panel state) in sync with the URL, ensuring that if the page is refreshed or a link is shared, the same plan detail reopens in the central overlay.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: No visual rendering or space usage; it operates purely in the background, influencing which plan-list-item is treated as focused and whether the plan-detail-panel is displayed.

name: Plan Builder
title: Plan Builder
description: Configure a new Bible reading plan or adjust reminder settings for an existing plan, including content scope, duration, schedule, and daily notification time.

core_features: Initialize the builder based on URL parameters (mode and optional planId), and load default options using getReadingPlanBuilderDefaults.
In create mode, allow the user to define plan basics: name, optional description, content scope, duration in days, chapters per day, and start date, using defaults from getReadingPlanBuilderDefaults.
In create mode, allow the user to configure daily reminders (enabled/disabled and time) using defaults from getReadingPlanBuilderDefaults.
In create mode, submit a new Bible reading plan using createBibleReadingPlan and redirect back to plans.html with focusPlanId set to the created plan’s id.
In edit mode, load an existing plan and its generated schedule using getReadingPlanDetail, displaying plan metadata and daily readings as read-only context.
In edit mode, allow the user to update only the daily reminder settings (enabled/disabled and time) for the existing plan using updateBibleReadingPlanNotifications.

Provide validation and error handling for required fields (e.g., plan name, content scope, duration, start date; reminder time when reminders are enabled) before calling createBibleReadingPlan or updateBibleReadingPlanNotifications.
Navigate back to plans.html after successful creation or reminder update, passing focusPlanId in the URL so the originating Plans page can highlight the relevant plan.
user_workflows:
Create a new Bible reading plan:
1. User navigates to plan_builder.html with mode=create in the URL (optionally without specifying mode, in which case the page treats it as create mode).
2. On page load, the Plan Builder calls getReadingPlanBuilderDefaults to retrieve available content scopes, default duration, default chapters per day, default start date, and default reminder settings.
3. Once defaults load, the plan details form is initialized: name is empty, description is empty, content scope options are populated from available_content_scopes, and duration/chapters/start date fields are set from the corresponding default values. Reminder settings (enabled flag and time) are also populated from the defaults.
4. The user fills in the required plan fields (name, content scope, duration in days, chapters per day, start date) and optionally the description. The user can toggle the daily reminder on or off and, if enabled, choose a reminder time.
5. As the user types or changes inputs, the component validates required fields locally (e.g., required fields not empty, numeric fields in acceptable ranges, reminder time present if reminders are enabled). Any validation issues are shown inline and prevent submission.
6. The user clicks the primary action button (e.g., “Create Plan”), triggering form submission. The page re-validates the inputs and, if valid, constructs the payload for createBibleReadingPlan using the current form state (name, description, contentScope, durationDays, chaptersPerDay, startDate, dailyReminderEnabled, dailyReminderTime).
7. The page calls createBibleReadingPlan. While waiting, the page disables inputs and the primary button and can show a loading state.
8. When createBibleReadingPlan returns, the page checks the success flag. If success is true and a plan with an id is returned, the page navigates the browser to plans.html with focusPlanId set to the returned plan.id as a URL parameter. If success is false or an error occurs, an error message is displayed and the user remains on the builder to correct inputs or retry.

Edit reminder settings for an existing reading plan:
1. User navigates to plan_builder.html with mode=edit and a planId URL parameter coming from plans.html.
2. On page load, the Plan Builder parses the URL. Because mode=edit and planId is present, it calls both getReadingPlanBuilderDefaults (for content scope options and potential defaults) and getReadingPlanDetail with the given planId.
3. When getReadingPlanDetail returns, the component populates the plan details form fields from the plan object (name, description, content_scope, duration_days, chapters_per_day, start_date). These fields are displayed as read-only since there is no update interface for them. The days list from the response is displayed in a schedule summary component for context (e.g., showing day numbers, assigned passages, and completion status) without allowing edits.
4. The reminder settings form is initialized from the plan’s daily_reminder_enabled and daily_reminder_time fields. The user can toggle the reminder on or off and adjust the time.
5. The user modifies the reminder settings. The component validates that when reminders are enabled, a reminder time is present and in a valid time format.
6. The user clicks the primary action button (e.g., “Save Changes”). The page validates the reminder fields; if valid, it calls updateBibleReadingPlanNotifications with the current reminder state (planId, dailyReminderEnabled, dailyReminderTime).
7. While waiting for the update response, the page disables the reminder inputs and primary button and may show a saving indicator.
8. When updateBibleReadingPlanNotifications returns with success=true and an updated plan, the page navigates to plans.html with focusPlanId set to the same planId so the updated plan can be highlighted. If success=false or the request fails, an error message is displayed and the reminder form remains visible to allow correction or retry.

Handle invalid or missing URL parameters:
1. User accesses plan_builder.html without mode or with an unsupported mode value. The initialization logic treats this as equivalent to mode=create and proceeds to call getReadingPlanBuilderDefaults, initializing an empty new-plan form.
2. User accesses plan_builder.html with mode=edit but without a planId parameter. The initialization logic detects the missing planId and displays a blocking error state indicating that the plan cannot be loaded. The plan and reminder forms are disabled, and no calls to getReadingPlanDetail or updateBibleReadingPlanNotifications are made.
3. User accesses plan_builder.html with mode=edit and a planId that cannot be loaded (e.g., getReadingPlanDetail returns an error or no plan). The page shows an error message and does not allow saving. The user can navigate back to plans.html using a secondary link or browser back.
4. In all error cases, no createBibleReadingPlan or updateBibleReadingPlanNotifications calls are made until required context (mode and valid planId when editing) is available.
interactions: On page load, the initialization component reads the mode and planId URL parameters and decides which interfaces to call: getReadingPlanBuilderDefaults for all modes, and getReadingPlanDetail only when mode=edit with a valid planId.

Changing the content scope field in create mode updates local form state and is constrained to the options provided by getReadingPlanBuilderDefaults.available_content_scopes; the selection is later passed as contentScope to createBibleReadingPlan.
Changing duration_days, chapters_per_day, or start_date in create mode updates local state; these values are later sent to createBibleReadingPlan as durationDays, chaptersPerDay, and startDate.
Typing into the name and description fields in create mode updates local form state; the name is required and validated before calling createBibleReadingPlan.

Toggling the daily reminder switch in either mode updates local reminder state (dailyReminderEnabled). If toggled on, the reminder time input is enabled and required; if toggled off, the reminder time input is disabled and excluded or null when calling the relevant interface.

Updating the daily reminder time control (e.g., time picker or text input constrained to a time format) updates local reminderTime state; this value is passed to createBibleReadingPlan in create mode or to updateBibleReadingPlanNotifications in edit mode.

Clicking the primary action button in create mode triggers form validation for all required fields and, if valid, calls createBibleReadingPlan with the current form state. On success, the page programmatically navigates to plans.html with focusPlanId set to the returned plan.id.

Clicking the primary action button in edit mode validates the reminder fields and, if valid, calls updateBibleReadingPlanNotifications with the current reminder state. On success, the page programmatically navigates to plans.html with focusPlanId set to the existing planId.

Clicking a secondary navigation control (such as a cancel link) returns the user to plans.html without calling createBibleReadingPlan or updateBibleReadingPlanNotifications, preserving the original URL parameters of the Plans page if they are included in the link.

Any API error (network failure or a success=false response) triggers display of an error message component with retry capability; the forms remain populated with the user’s inputs so they can adjust and resubmit.
page_functionality.state_logic:
The page state is driven primarily by URL parameters and API responses rather than localStorage. On initialization, the component reads the query parameters from the current URL. The mode parameter determines whether the page behaves as a creator (mode=create) or an editor (mode=edit). If mode is absent or not equal to the expected values, the page defaults to create mode to allow constructing a new plan.

In create mode, only getReadingPlanBuilderDefaults is called. The response initializes in-memory state for: available content scopes, default duration_days, default chapters_per_day, default start_date, and default reminder settings. These default values seed the form fields but can be overridden by the user. The create form’s state (plan name, description, content scope, duration, chapters per day, start date, reminder enabled flag, and reminder time) exists only in memory for the lifespan of the page and is used to construct the payload for createBibleReadingPlan. After a successful creation, the page constructs a URL for plans.html with a focusPlanId parameter equal to the created plan’s id returned by createBibleReadingPlan and navigates the browser there.

In edit mode, a valid planId URL parameter is required. The page calls getReadingPlanBuilderDefaults to obtain global options (such as content scopes) and getReadingPlanDetail with the planId to fetch the specific plan and its days. Once both responses are available, the page holds two main in-memory states: (1) the read-only plan details and schedule (populated from the plan and days objects returned by getReadingPlanDetail); and (2) the editable reminder state (daily_reminder_enabled and daily_reminder_time). Only the reminder state is mutable by the user. When the user saves changes, the current reminder state is sent to updateBibleReadingPlanNotifications along with the planId. On success, the page navigates to plans.html with focusPlanId equal to that same planId.

Error states (such as missing planId in edit mode, unavailable plan, or API failures) are represented in memory and control conditional rendering: for example, showing an error banner and disabling forms. No state is persisted via localStorage or other client-side storage; the authoritative state always comes from the URL parameters and the most recent API responses. Navigation away from the page (e.g., back to plans.html) is implemented by constructing URLs with the appropriate query parameters (not by storing navigation context elsewhere).

components.functionality: Parses URL parameters (mode and planId), determines the page mode (create or edit), triggers loading of defaults via getReadingPlanBuilderDefaults, and when in edit mode also triggers loading of plan details and schedule via getReadingPlanDetail. Manages global loading and error states that gate the rendering of subsequent components.

Collects and displays the core fields of a Bible reading plan: name, optional description, content scope, duration in days, chapters per day, and start date. In create mode, fields are editable and initialized from getReadingPlanBuilderDefaults. In edit mode, fields are populated from getReadingPlanDetail.plan and rendered as read-only, serving as contextual info but not editable since only notifications can be updated through the available interfaces.

Manages reminder configuration for the plan. Presents a toggle for enabling/disabling daily reminders and a time input for the reminder time. In create mode, the initial values come from getReadingPlanBuilderDefaults and are included in the payload sent to createBibleReadingPlan by the save actions component. In edit mode, the initial values come from getReadingPlanDetail.plan and updates are persisted via updateBibleReadingPlanNotifications when the user saves changes.

Displays a read-only summary of the plan’s daily schedule, using the days array from getReadingPlanDetail. Each item shows key fields such as day_number, scheduled date (if present), passages, and completion status. This component is only active in edit mode, providing context for the existing plan; it does not allow creating, editing, or deleting days because no such interface is available.

Surfaces status and error messages related to API operations. Listens to the loader and save actions components and displays loading, success, or error messages when getReadingPlanBuilderDefaults, getReadingPlanDetail, createBibleReadingPlan, or updateBibleReadingPlanNotifications are called or resolved. Does not store data itself but reflects the current status for user feedback.

Provides primary and secondary actions for the Plan Builder. In create mode, clicking the primary action triggers validation of the plan-details-form and reminder-settings-form, then calls createBibleReadingPlan with the aggregated form data. On a successful response, it redirects to plans.html with focusPlanId set to the new plan’s id. In edit mode, clicking the primary action validates reminder fields and calls updateBibleReadingPlanNotifications with the current planId and reminder settings, then redirects to plans.html with focusPlanId set to the existing planId on success. The secondary action navigates back to plans.html without performing any API calls.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single central column likely mapped to a standard 12-column grid, with all visible content centered within a full-width hero section.
layout.layout_pattern: All Plan Builder components sit in a single, centered vertical column over a full-bleed devotional background (e.g., soft sunset/gradient) that echoes the hero section from the mockup. Components are stacked in a clear top-to-bottom sequence: status banner at the very top of the content column, followed by the main plan details form, then the reminder settings, the plan days summary, and finally the save actions bar at the bottom. The loader temporarily overlays everything, centered on the screen, but does not change the underlying document flow. There are no sidebars or multi-column layouts; everything aligns to one central axis.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All Plan Builder components sit in a single, centered vertical column over a full-bleed devotional background (e.g., soft sunset/gradient) that echoes the hero section from the mockup. Components are stacked in a clear top-to-bottom sequence: status banner at the very top of the content column, followed by the main plan details form, then the reminder settings, the plan days summary, and finally the save actions bar at the bottom. The loader temporarily overlays everything, centered on the screen, but does not change the underlying document flow. There are no sidebars or multi-column layouts; everything aligns to one central axis.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top 20–25% of the viewport is reserved for initial messaging and context, represented here by the status banner and the upper part of the plan details form, giving the feel of a hero header. The central 40–50% of the viewport is where the bulk of interactive content lives (plan details, reminder settings, days summary), treated as large, airy cards within the central column. The remaining lower portion of the screen is used for the actions bar and additional breathing room, ensuring the bottom of the page is not visually crowded. Horizontally, the central column spans about 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop, with generous margins left and right so the background image frames the content.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content is deliberately sparse and airy. Each major component (forms, data list, actions) is treated as its own block with 24–32px internal padding and 48–64px vertical spacing between blocks, echoing the open, meditative feel of the hero. Only a few major elements are visible at any scroll position, and text lines are kept at a comfortable width to avoid dense, cluttered fields. Where a component has multiple controls (like forms), fields are grouped and separated with internal spacing that respects the 8px base unit and favors clarity and calm over compactness.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye starts near the top of the central column with the status banner strip (when present), then moves down to the main plan details form, which serves as the page’s primary focal area. From there, the flow continues downward through the reminder settings and into the days summary, which acts as a preview or confirmation of the configuration. Finally, attention is drawn to the actions bar at the bottom where the user commits their choices. A consistent center alignment and vertically stacked layout reinforce a strong, linear top-to-bottom reading order, similar to the hero’s nav → title → scripture progression from the mockup.

layout.overall_description: The Plan Builder page sits on top of a full-width, full-height devotional background that evokes the original hero image: warm, soft tones with the edges left intentionally clear. In front of this, a single, centered column acts as the structural spine for all content, roughly 60–70% of the viewport wide on larger screens, narrowing on smaller devices while always staying centered. At the very top of this column, a slim status banner, when shown, spans the column and sets the initial tone, much like the subtle navigation strip in the hero. Immediately below, the main plan details form appears as a prominent, softly elevated card with generous padding, functioning as the equivalent of the hero’s central title block. Below it, the reminder settings and the days summary continue the vertical rhythm, each as distinct yet visually related panels. Large vertical gaps between these blocks allow the background to breathe around them, preserving the low-density, reverent feel. At the bottom of the column, the save actions bar anchors the interaction, centered and clearly separated from the content above with ample whitespace, so the page feels grounded but never cramped. Over all of this, when loading, a centered overlay loader temporarily becomes the primary focal point while still hinting at the serene layout beneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The loader appears as a full-viewport overlay that sits above the devotional background and all other components. It covers the entire width and height of the screen with a semi-transparent dark veil (around 40–60 percent opacity) that still allows the warm background and the suggestion of the central column beneath to faintly show through. In the absolute center of the viewport, a circular spinner or subtle loading indicator is placed, with a small, centered loading label directly beneath it. The loader’s content is centered both vertically and horizontally, mirroring the centered scripture block in the hero and creating a calm focal point while data is being prepared. No other components are visually interactive while this overlay is active.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay that covers the entire viewport while active, positioned above all other components with no scroll interaction; underlying layout remains in place but is visually and functionally blocked.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The API status banner is positioned at the very top of the Plan Builder content column, just below any global site navigation, acting like a slim, centered horizontal strip. It spans the full width of the central column (approximately 60–70 percent of the viewport width on desktop, capped at a comfortable max width), with equal left and right margins letting the background image frame it. Vertically, it is compact, occupying roughly 48–64px in height, with at least 48–64px of breathing room above the next component. Its content is center-aligned: an icon and text sit on the horizontal center line, making it visually similar in placement to the hero’s top navigation band but less dominant than the main form. When no status is needed, this strip collapses completely, and the plan details form moves up to occupy the top of the column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element in the normal document flow at the top of the central column; it pushes subsequent components downward when visible and leaves extra whitespace when hidden.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan details form is the primary structural block in the page and sits directly below the API status banner (or directly beneath the top spacing if no banner is present). Horizontally, it is centered on the page, aligning with the central column and spanning about 60–70 percent of the viewport width on desktop, with a max width around a typical content container so text lines remain readable. The form is presented as a card or panel with a soft background (solid or slightly translucent over the hero image), rounded corners, and generous internal padding of roughly 24–32px on all sides, maintaining the airy, devotional feel. There is a vertical gap of 64px above it to separate it from the top edge of the content area and at least 48–64px of whitespace below it before the next component. Internally, the form’s title and any iconography are centered or aligned along the central axis of the page, echoing the hero’s large brand statement and cross, while the individual fields can be left-aligned within that card for readability. The overall height of the form typically occupies about one third to one half of the viewport height on initial load, depending on the number of fields, ensuring it feels substantial but not overwhelming.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable content block within the central column; it defines the main focal area upon load and sets the horizontal alignment for all subsequent components.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reminder settings form-section is placed directly below the plan details form, with a comfortable vertical gap of about 48–64px between the bottom of the plan details card and the top of this section. It shares the same horizontal alignment and width as the plan details form, centered on the page and spanning the same 60–70 percent of the viewport width, reinforcing the single-column rhythm. Visually, it reads as a slightly smaller sibling card: similar background, rounded corners, and internal padding around 24px, but with a bit less vertical height since it typically contains fewer fields. This positioning maintains the hero-like flow: primary configuration (plan details) first, then secondary configuration (reminders) next in the vertical stack. There is clear whitespace to the left and right where only the background image is visible, preserving the open, reverent framing. Below this form, another 48–64px of vertical spacing separates it from the plan days summary, so each section feels distinct and easy to scan.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block that follows the plan details form in the single-column flow; it scrolls naturally with the page and maintains consistent alignment with the other cards.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan days summary data list sits beneath the reminder settings form as a preview-oriented panel, maintaining the same centered alignment and width as the previous components. It begins approximately 48–64px below the bottom edge of the reminder section, creating a clear separation. The panel spans the full width of the central column, again around 60–70 percent of the viewport width, and uses internal padding of roughly 24px to keep the list content airy. Whether it is rendered as rows, pills, or a simple textual list, the items are organized vertically and centered within the card, echoing the scripture block’s centered presence in the hero but with more structured data styling. Its height is flexible and can grow with the number of days displayed, but ample whitespace is preserved above and below so that, even when tall, it still feels like a distinct, breathable block framed by the background. A vertical gap of about 64px below this component leads into the actions bar area, marking the transition from configuration/preview to final commitment.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, scrollable panel within the central column that expands vertically as needed while keeping its top edge aligned to the overall vertical rhythm of the page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The plan save actions bar is positioned at the bottom of the Plan Builder content column, directly beneath the plan days summary with a generous vertical gap of approximately 64px to distinguish it as the final step. Horizontally, it is centered on the same axis as all other components. The bar itself spans slightly less or equal to the central column width (for example, 50–60 percent of the viewport on desktop), so the primary actions sit comfortably in the visual center with clear margins on both sides, allowing the background to frame them. Buttons within the bar are grouped tightly but surrounded by sufficient padding (16–24px vertically and horizontally within the bar) so they feel substantial and easy to tap or click. The area below the actions retains at least 64–96px of vertical whitespace to keep the bottom of the page from feeling cramped, echoing the open lower portion of the original hero. On longer pages, as the user scrolls, this actions bar can become sticky near the bottom edge of the viewport, ensuring constant access to Save while still visually belonging to the central column rather than reading as a global footer.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Initially a static element at the bottom of the central column; once scrolled into view, it can adopt a sticky position near the bottom of the viewport within the column’s horizontal bounds, staying visible as the user reviews content above.

name: Prayer & Requests
title: Prayer Requests
description: Page where visitors can learn how the ministry prays and submit a prayer request with category, anonymity preference, and optional weekly prayer newsletter opt-in.

core_features: Load and display explanatory content about the prayer ministry and how requests are used, using getPrayerPageContent.
Display a primary “Submit a Prayer Request” action that scrolls or jumps focus to the request form.
Render a prayer request form that loads available categories using getPrayerRequestCategoryOptions and optionally pre-selects a category based on the prefillCategory URL parameter.
Capture the user’s prayer request text and an anonymity preference flag.
Offer an opt-in checkbox for the weekly prayer newsletter, including an email field passed to submitPrayerRequest.
Submit the prayer request via submitPrayerRequest, handle success and error states, and display a confirmation message based on the API response.
Provide a navigation link from the confirmation state back to the home page (index.html) with a prayer highlight parameter in the URL.
user_workflows:
Workflow 1: Open page from navigation and submit a request
1. User navigates to prayer.html via the main menu or footer.
2. The page controller calls getPrayerPageContent to retrieve intro_html, guidelines_html, and newsletter_description.
3. In parallel, the page controller calls getPrayerRequestCategoryOptions to retrieve the list of category options.
4. Once content loads, the info section displays the intro and guidelines, and the form displays with an initial category selected (e.g., the first loaded category) and empty fields.
5. User clicks the “Submit a Prayer Request” button in the page body, which scrolls or jumps focus to the prayer request form component.
6. User selects a category from the dynamically loaded list, types their prayer request text, sets anonymity preference, and optionally checks the weekly prayer newsletter opt-in checkbox and enters an email.
7. User clicks the form’s submit button. The form runs client-side validation (e.g., required category, non-empty text, email format if newsletter is selected).
8. On passing validation, the form component calls submitPrayerRequest with category, text, isAnonymous, newsletterOptIn, and email.
9. While waiting, the form shows a submitting state and disables inputs.
10. If submitPrayerRequest returns success = true, the page shows a confirmation component using the returned message and prayer_request data and hides or disables the form.
11. From the confirmation component, the user can either click a “Back to Home” link, which navigates to index.html?highlight=prayer, or choose to submit another request, which resets the form state.
Workflow 2: Arrive from another page with a preselected category
1. User clicks a link on index.html or groups.html that points to prayer.html with a prefillCategory URL parameter (for example, prayer.html?prefillCategory=someCategoryCode).
2. The prayer page controller parses the prefillCategory value from the URL upon load.
3. The controller calls getPrayerPageContent and getPrayerRequestCategoryOptions.
4. When category options are returned, the controller checks whether prefillCategory matches one of the category.value options.
5. If a match is found, that category is set as the initial selected value in the prayer request form; otherwise, the form falls back to its default category selection logic.
6. The user reviews the pre-selected category, updates it if desired, fills in the request text, anonymity preference, and optional newsletter opt-in and email.
7. User submits the form, which calls submitPrayerRequest and then follows the same confirmation flow as in Workflow 1.
Workflow 3: Submit without newsletter vs. with newsletter
1. User fills out the form, chooses category, and enters prayer request text.
2. Case A (no newsletter): User leaves the newsletter opt-in checkbox unchecked. The form may still allow email input but treat newsletterOptIn as false when calling submitPrayerRequest. The submitPrayerRequest call sends newsletterOptIn = false and the email (if provided). The response may include a newsletter_subscription object in a non-subscribed state, which is not emphasized to the user.
3. Case B (with newsletter): User checks the newsletter opt-in. The email field is validated as required and must be in valid format before submission. The form calls submitPrayerRequest with newsletterOptIn = true and the provided email. On success, the confirmation component can indicate that the user has been added to the weekly prayer newsletter, using the returned newsletter_subscription data or the generic message from the API.
4. In both cases, success and error handling is the same: success shows the confirmation panel; a failure shows an error banner and keeps the form data so the user can retry.
interactions:
Page load interactions:
- On initial load of prayer.html, the controller reads the prefillCategory value from the current URL’s query string.
- The controller triggers two async calls: getPrayerPageContent and getPrayerRequestCategoryOptions.
- While loading, the page may show loading indicators for the content and the form. If either call fails, an error state is shown with a retry action that re-calls the respective interface.
Content section interactions:
- The info section displays intro_html and guidelines_html returned from getPrayerPageContent.
- No editing is possible; this is read-only informational content.
- The section may contain internal anchors (provided by intro_html/guidelines_html) that the user can click; these are handled by the browser and do not require additional APIs.
Submit a Prayer Request button interactions:
- A global button within the page content is labeled as the primary call-to-action.
- onClick: prevents any default navigation, finds the prayer request form component by an internal anchor or DOM ID, and scrolls/focuses it.
- This button does not call any external interface directly; it simply helps the user reach the form quickly.
Prayer request form interactions:
- Category select:
  - Options are populated entirely from getPrayerRequestCategoryOptions.categories.
  - onChange: updates the form’s selectedCategory state.
- Prefill logic:
  - When categories load, if a prefillCategory parameter exists and matches an option’s value, the selectedCategory is initialized to that option.
  - If no match, selectedCategory is initialized according to a default rule, such as the first available category in the list.
- Request text field:
  - onInput/onChange: updates requestText state.
  - Client-side validation ensures this is non-empty before submit.
- Anonymity preference:
  - Implemented as either a checkbox or a toggle.
  - onChange: updates isAnonymous state (boolean) used in submitPrayerRequest.
- Newsletter opt-in checkbox:
  - onChange: updates newsletterOptIn state.
