- View-only changes (sort, order, page, page_size, reorder): These update only client-side derived state. The underlying items from getWatchLaterList are not changed, and no API calls are made.
- Removal: After removeSermonFromWatchLater returns success, the controller removes that sermon from the underlying in-memory items and re-applies the current URL-based derivation (sort, pagination). If the current page becomes empty (e.g., last item removed from the last page), the controller may adjust 'page' downwards and update the URL accordingly to show the previous page.
- Reorder: When reorder mode is on (reorder=1), the controller disregards any sort parameter other than 'position' to ensure that the current visual order reflects the persisted order. Dragging or moving items modifies the order of the in-memory items. When Save order is triggered and reorderWatchLaterItems succeeds, the controller optionally re-calls getWatchLaterList to refresh positions, then reapplies any view parameters. If the user cancels reorder mode, the controller resets the in-memory order to the last known persisted order (from the initial or refreshed getWatchLaterList call) and updates the reorder parameter to indicate normal mode.
- Navigation: Navigating away from the page and back (including via bookmarks with specific URL parameters) will always cause a fresh getWatchLaterList call to ensure the list reflects the current server state.

components.functionality: Coordinates data loading and overall state for the Watch Later page. On load, calls getWatchLaterList, reads URL parameters (sort, order, page, page_size, reorder), derives the visible subset of items, and passes data to child components. It also reacts to URL changes, handles successful calls to removeSermonFromWatchLater and reorderWatchLaterItems, and may re-call getWatchLaterList after a successful reorder to refresh positions.

Displays the list_name from getWatchLaterList and the current total number of items (using the in-memory items length or total_watch_later_count after removals). Provides a Reorder mode toggle that updates the reorder URL parameter and notifies the page controller to enter or exit reorder mode.

Allows the user to adjust how the Watch Later items are presented (in non-reorder mode). Provides controls to select sort field (position, title, date_published, duration_minutes, rating_average) and sort direction (asc/desc). Updates the sort and order URL parameters and informs the page controller to recompute the derived list. This component is disabled or constrained when reorder mode is active so that position-based order remains authoritative.

Renders the current page of Watch Later items as derived by the page controller. For each item, shows data from getWatchLaterList (title, thumbnail_url, duration_minutes, rating_average, date_published, and implied position) and exposes interactions: click to open the sermon detail page, click to remove from Watch Later, and drag/move controls for reordering when reorder mode is active.

Represents a single Watch Later entry. Binds to a single item from getWatchLaterList, including watch_later_item_id and sermon_id. Handles navigation to sermon_detail.html?id={sermon_id} when the main row area is clicked. Handles removal via removeSermonFromWatchLater(sermonId) when the Remove control is clicked. In reorder mode, exposes a drag handle or move-up/move-down controls and emits reordering events to the list component and page controller.

Visible when reorder mode is active. Tracks whether the current in-memory order of Watch Later items differs from the last persisted order (dirty state). Provides actions to Save order and Cancel changes. On Save, compiles the ordered list of watch_later_item_id values from the current items and calls reorderWatchLaterItems with orderedWatchLaterItemIds. On Cancel, instructs the page controller to revert to the last persisted order and updates the reorder URL parameter.

Controls which subset of the in-memory Watch Later items is shown. Uses the current total count and URL parameters (page, page_size) from the page controller to render navigation (e.g., next/previous page, specific page numbers). When the user changes page or page_size, it updates the corresponding URL parameters and notifies the controller to recompute the visible items; no new API calls are made.

Shown when the in-memory list of Watch Later items is empty (either initially or after removals). Communicates that there are no sermons currently saved to watch later. May offer a simple link back to other parts of the site (e.g., the main sermons listing page) implemented purely as a navigation link without additional API calls.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single central column likely mapped to a standard 12-column grid, with all visible content centered within a full-width hero section.
layout.layout_pattern: All components sit in a single, centered vertical column over a full-bleed photographic background (continuing the warm sunset/spiritual hero style). The flow is strictly top-to-bottom: page controller defines the centered column, then the summary header (page title/description), followed by the sort toolbar and reorder controls as a light control band, then the main list of sermons (or empty state if there are none), and finally pagination at the bottom. No sidebars or multi-column layouts; all controls and content are stacked and center-aligned within the main column, echoing the mockup’s linear hero structure.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components sit in a single, centered vertical column over a full-bleed photographic background (continuing the warm sunset/spiritual hero style). The flow is strictly top-to-bottom: page controller defines the centered column, then the summary header (page title/description), followed by the sort toolbar and reorder controls as a light control band, then the main list of sermons (or empty state if there are none), and finally pagination at the bottom. No sidebars or multi-column layouts; all controls and content are stacked and center-aligned within the main column, echoing the mockup’s linear hero structure.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The upper ~20–25% of the viewport is used by the summary header, giving the page title space similar to the hero’s brand/title zone. The central ~40–50% of the viewport is reserved for the list (or empty state), which sits over the darker mid-band of the background for readability, echoing the scripture placement in the mockup. The remaining vertical space below is used for pagination and breathing room so the photograph remains visible. Horizontally, the main content column spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport on desktop (max-width ~960–1040px), centered, with generous left/right margins letting the background image frame the content.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content remains relatively sparse and airy. Each block (header, toolbar, list, pagination) is separated by 24–64px of vertical space, with inner padding of 16–24px per component to maintain an open, devotional feel. The list rows are clearly separated but not crowded; there are fewer, larger elements rather than many small controls. Text blocks avoid edge-to-edge congestion, mirroring the hero’s generous breathing space around the scripture.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye is guided top-to-bottom similar to the original hero: first to the summary header (page title acting like the hero’s main brand statement), then down to the control band (sort + reorder) as a subtle secondary layer, and then into the central list or empty state, which occupies the visual ‘sunburst’ center of the page. Pagination comes last as a quiet navigational element. Background tonal contrast (slightly darker mid-section) supports readability at the list area, while lighter top and bottom allow the photograph to frame the content and emphasize the central portion of the page.

layout.overall_description: The Watch Later page sits on the same full-bleed, warm devotional background used across the site. A translucent, centered content column floats above this image, aligned to the vertical middle but biased slightly upward, echoing the hero layout. At the very top of this column, the summary header claims the upper fifth of the viewport: a reverent serif page title in larger type, perhaps with a small cross motif, followed by a calm, sans-serif line describing how many sermons are saved. Beneath it, with a comfortable gap, a slim horizontal control band houses sorting and reordering tools, kept visually light so they don’t compete with the title. The heart of the page is the list area directly below: a stack of softly delineated sermon rows sitting over the darker mid-tone of the background, mirroring how scripture text floated in the center of the hero mockup. Each row has ample vertical padding and space between entries, so the list feels contemplative rather than utilitarian. If there are no sermons, a single centered empty-state card takes over this central region, becoming the focal point much like the scripture block in the hero. At the bottom of the column, pagination sits quietly, centered with generous spacing from the list and page bottom, allowing the background to re-emerge and giving the layout an open, unhurried finish.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page controller defines a full-bleed hero-style canvas: a 100vh (or taller) section with the warm sunset background image filling the entire viewport width and height. On top of this, it establishes a single centered content column (max-width ~960–1040px, typically 70–80% of viewport width on desktop) aligned horizontally to the center and vertically starting just below the global site navigation/branding. It applies generous vertical padding at the top (48–64px from the bottom edge of the global header to the summary header) and bottom (64–96px to the page end) to keep the content floating within the image and to mirror the openness of the hero. The controller ensures all other components (header, toolbar, list/empty state, pagination) flow in a simple top-to-bottom order within this column, with consistent vertical spacing multiples (24–64px) between them. On smaller screens, it slightly increases vertical stacking (more spacing between blocks) while letting the column width shrink to ~90–95% of the viewport to maintain a centered, framed feel.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Acts as the main structural wrapper in normal document flow, stretching to at least full viewport height. It maintains a fixed full-bleed background while the inner content column scrolls, similar to a hero section that extends into a content area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The summary header sits at the top of the content column, occupying roughly the upper 20–25% of the viewport height on initial load. It is horizontally centered within the column and uses a stacked layout: the page title (‘Watch Later’ or equivalent) in a large, all-caps or small-caps serif aligned center, possibly with a small gold cross icon above or beside it, followed by a single-line sans-serif description such as the count of saved sermons or a short explanatory phrase. The block spans about 60–70% of the content column width, so there is generous margin on both left and right, leaving the background visible. Vertical padding inside the header block is around 24–32px top and bottom, with 8–16px between title and description, reinforcing the calm, reverent tone. The header content sits over the lighter-to-mid tone area of the background, ensuring strong legibility and recreating the sense of a hero title band at the top of the page.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Remains in normal scroll flow; it appears just below the global navigation and scrolls away as the user moves down the page, acting like an inner-page hero rather than a sticky header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort toolbar appears immediately beneath the summary header, separated by about 24–32px of vertical space. It is a low-profile, horizontal strip centered within the main column, spanning about 70–80% of the column’s width. Controls (e.g., a sort dropdown, filter chip, or a ‘Clear all’ action) are arranged on a single row. On larger screens, the primary sort control is centered or slightly left-of-center while still maintaining overall visual centering of the row; the rest of the controls align in a balanced manner with even horizontal spacing. On smaller screens, the controls wrap to a second line if necessary, but remain center-aligned. The toolbar has subtle visual separation from the background—either a faint translucent panel or just the underlying column spacing—without becoming a heavy bar. Inner vertical padding is 12–16px, maintaining the airy aesthetic while clearly grouping the sorting interactions as a distinct layer between the header and the list.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Typically scrolls with the page in normal document flow. On larger screens it may be optionally configured as a sticky element within the column (sticking to the top of the viewport once scrolled into place) to keep sort controls accessible without breaking the centered, single-column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The list view occupies the central portion of the page, starting about 24–32px below the sort toolbar. It fills most of the remaining vertical space in the viewport (approximately 40–50% of the viewport height in view on load), sitting over the darker, mid-tone band of the background image to echo how the scripture text occupied the hero’s center. The list is a vertical stack of watch-later item rows, all aligned flush left within the content column but visually centered within the page because the column itself is centered. The list’s usable width matches the content column (up to ~960px), with each row spanning nearly the full column width and maintaining 16–24px of inner padding. Between rows, there is a vertical gap of 16–24px, giving each sermon space to breathe. The list is visually grouped as a single, continuous region: this can be achieved through a subtle shared background (e.g., a faint translucent panel) behind all rows or simply through consistent spacing that makes the mid-page area feel like one coherent content block.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Rendered in normal document flow as a scrollable stack of rows within the page. The list itself does not create a separate scroll container; instead, the entire page scrolls, maintaining the hero-like continuity of background and layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Each item row is a horizontally-oriented card within the list, visually similar in weight to a line of scripture: clear, readable, and given room to stand alone. The row spans nearly 100% of the content column width, with 16–24px of internal padding on all sides. It is aligned left within the column but remains vertically centered within the list stack. A typical row height is around 96–136px on desktop, depending on content (thumbnail, title, speaker, duration, and action buttons), allowing titles to wrap to two lines without feeling cramped. Content within the row arranges linearly: left side may hold a small thumbnail or icon, central area the sermon title and metadata, and the right side the key actions (e.g., ‘Watch now,’ remove from list, drag/reorder handle) aligned to the right edge. Vertically, the text and actions are centered within the row’s height for a calm, stable presence. There is a separation of 16–24px between rows, so each feels like a distinct devotional entry. The visual treatment is light: perhaps a subtle shadow or a soft translucent background to distinguish the row from the hero image while preserving the spiritual, non-cluttered atmosphere.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Lives inside the list’s normal flow, stacking vertically with consistent gaps. When reordering is enabled, the row may show a drag handle, but its position and size do not alter the row’s overall footprint or the centered column layout.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The reorder actions are placed close to the sort toolbar to form a unified ‘controls band’ above the list. On larger screens, these actions (e.g., a toggle to enter reorder mode, or buttons like ‘Move up/down mode’ or ‘Reset order’) are aligned to the right edge of the same horizontal area that contains the sort toolbar, giving a balanced top band: sort tools leaning center/left, reorder tools on the right, all contained within the same visual strip spanning ~70–80% of the column. A small horizontal gap (16–24px) separates them visually but they share the same vertical alignment. On smaller screens, when horizontal space is constrained, the reorder actions drop onto a second line directly beneath the sort controls, center-aligned to match the single-column feel; there is a small vertical gap of 8–12px between the lines. The controls themselves are understated (text buttons or small icons with labels) to avoid overpowering the page title and list; they serve as functional accents rather than focal points.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Grouped with the sort toolbar in the upper control band of the content column, scrolling normally with the page and inheriting any optional sticky behavior that might be applied to the toolbar on larger screens.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Pagination controls sit at the bottom of the content column, directly below the list, separated from the final item row by a generous 32–48px gap. They are horizontally centered and occupy a compact band spanning about 40–60% of the column width, so the surrounding hero background remains visible. The pagination elements (page numbers, next/previous arrows) form a single, centered row with 8–16px spacing between individual controls, echoing the calm rhythm of the rest of the layout. There is additional vertical padding—about 16–24px of internal spacing—to give the pagination bar breathing room. Below it, the page controller maintains 64–96px of clear space to the bottom of the viewport/content, allowing the background image to frame the pagination and giving the layout a gentle, unhurried ending.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears in normal document flow after the list (or hidden when the empty state is shown). It scrolls away like any other content and does not stick, keeping the visual emphasis on the central list and header.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty state takes the place of the list and pagination whenever there are no saved sermons. It is positioned in the central region of the page, starting roughly where the list would begin (about 24–32px below the control band) and extending vertically so that its main content (icon/illustration and text) sits near the visual center of the viewport—the same zone where the scripture passage appears in the original hero mockup. The empty-state card is centered within the content column and spans about 60–70% of the column width, leaving generous margins at the sides for the background image to show through. Inside, it uses a vertical stack: a simple, spiritually resonant icon (perhaps a subtle outline of a Bible or a play icon within a circle), a short, calm headline, and one or two lines of descriptive copy, all center-aligned. Vertical spacing between these elements uses 16–24px gaps, and the card has 24–32px padding top and bottom to feel substantial yet airy. This block becomes the primary focal point of the page when the list is empty, mirroring how the hero’s scripture is the centerpiece amid surrounding openness.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Displayed in normal document flow where the list would be; the list and pagination are hidden when the empty state is visible. It scrolls naturally with the page, but on initial load it typically appears within the central viewport window, reinforcing the hero-like central focus.

name: Store / Resources
title: Store Resources
description: Browse and filter ministry resources by category, price, and format, view product details, and add items to the cart.

core_features: Load and display store categories for navigation using getStoreCategories, with the current category determined by the categoryId URL parameter.
Load and display available filter options (price range, formats, types) for the active category using getProductFilterOptions.
List products for the active category with filters (maxPrice, format, type, isFree) and sorting (sortBy) applied using listProductsByCategory.
Visually distinguish free resources (is_free = true / price = 0) from paid products in the product list.
Allow users to change filters (price slider/input, format, type, free-only) and update the URL parameters so the product list can be reloaded with the new criteria.
Allow users to sort products (e.g., by price or bestselling) and navigate between pages of results using URL parameters sortBy and page.
Provide product cards that link to product_detail.html with the product ID and current categoryId to support a 'back to category' experience.
Enable adding products to the cart from the store page using addProductToCart and show immediate feedback (success, error, updated cart item count and subtotal).
Provide navigation to cart.html via a cart control so users can review and complete their order.
user_workflows:
Browse by category from header or footer:
1) User clicks a 'Store' or category link that opens store.html with a categoryId URL parameter.
2) On page load, the page reads categoryId and other URL parameters (maxPrice, format, type, isFree, sortBy, page).
3) The page calls getStoreCategories to display all active categories and highlight the one matching categoryId.
4) The page calls getProductFilterOptions with the active categoryId to initialize filter controls (price range, formats, types).
5) The page builds a filters object from URL parameters and calls listProductsByCategory with categoryId, filters, sort_by (mapped from sortBy), page, and a configured page_size.
6) The product grid displays the returned items, marking is_free items as free and showing stock/format details as appropriate.
7) User can switch to a different category via the category navigation; the page updates categoryId in the URL and reloads filters and products for the new category.

Apply filters for budget, format, and free resources:
1) User adjusts the maximum price control in the filter component; the component updates the maxPrice value in the URL query string and resets page to 1.
2) User toggles a 'Free only' control; the component sets isFree in the URL (e.g., isFree=true) and may remove or ignore maxPrice where appropriate.
3) User selects desired formats (e.g., physical, ebook, audio) from the filter controls; the component updates the format parameter in the URL (single value or a serialized representation if multiple formats are allowed).
4) User selects a resource type (e.g., book, devotional); the component updates the type parameter in the URL.
5) On each URL change, the page re-computes the filters object and calls listProductsByCategory again so results reflect the new filter combination.
6) The product grid updates to show only the resources that match the current filters.

Sort and paginate results:
1) User changes the sort order control (e.g., selecting price ascending or bestselling).
2) The sort component updates the sortBy parameter in the URL and resets page to 1.
3) The page calls listProductsByCategory with the updated sort_by value.
4) User uses pagination controls to move between pages; clicking a page number or next/previous updates the page parameter in the URL (page=1, page=2, etc.).
5) Each pagination action triggers a new call to listProductsByCategory with the same filters and sortBy but a different page, updating the product grid accordingly.

View product details:
1) User clicks on a product card image or title within the product grid.
2) The click handler reads the product_id from the clicked item and the current categoryId from the page state/URL.
3) The user is navigated to product_detail.html with URL parameters id (the clicked product's product_id) and categoryId (current categoryId) so that the detail page can offer a 'Back to category' link.
4) When the user later clicks 'Back to category' on the product detail page, they return to store.html with categoryId preserved; optionally, other filter/sort parameters can also be preserved by that page.

Add resources to cart:
1) User clicks an 'Add to cart' control on a product card or product row.
2) The add-to-cart component uses the selected quantity (defaulting to 1 if no quantity selector is present) and calls addProductToCart with productId set to the product's product_id and quantity set to the chosen quantity.
3) When addProductToCart returns, the component reads success, message, cart_item_count, and cart_subtotal from the response.
4) If success is true, the component displays a success message, updates a cart item count indicator on the page, and may show the updated subtotal.
5) If success is false, the component displays the returned message as an error (e.g., related to stock limits).
6) User can click a 'View Cart' or cart icon, which navigates to cart.html without additional parameters.

Access free devotional resources directly:
1) User clicks a footer or campaign link targeting free devotional resources that opens store.html with categoryId (for the appropriate category) and isFree=true in the URL.
2) On load, the page initializes with isFree=true; filters are set so that only free resources (is_free=true) are requested.
3) listProductsByCategory is called with filters including a free-only flag derived from isFree, and the grid displays only free items.
4) User may optionally adjust other filters (format/type) or clear the free-only filter, which updates isFree in the URL and reloads results accordingly.
interactions: Category navigation click: Clicking a category in the category navigation updates the categoryId URL parameter (and resets page, with optional clearing or preserving of filters depending on requirements) and triggers fresh calls to getProductFilterOptions and listProductsByCategory.

Filter change (max price): Changing the maximum price input or slider updates the maxPrice URL parameter, resets page to 1, and triggers a new listProductsByCategory request using the updated price filter.

Filter change (format): Selecting or deselecting a format value updates the format URL parameter and triggers a new listProductsByCategory request with the updated format included in the filters object.
Filter change (type): Selecting a resource type updates the type URL parameter and triggers a new listProductsByCategory request with the updated type filter.

Filter change (free-only): Toggling the free-only control updates the isFree URL parameter (e.g., isFree=true or removing it for false), resets page to 1, and triggers a new listProductsByCategory request that restricts results to free items when enabled.
Sorting: Selecting a sort option updates the sortBy URL parameter and triggers a new listProductsByCategory request with the new sort_by; the results in the product grid are reordered accordingly.

Pagination: Clicking on a page number or next/previous control updates the page URL parameter and triggers a new listProductsByCategory call for that page while preserving all other URL-defined filters.
Product detail navigation: Clicking a product card title or image navigates to product_detail.html with URL parameters id (the product's ID) and categoryId (from the current URL state).

Add to cart: Clicking 'Add to cart' on a product triggers addProductToCart with the product ID and chosen quantity; the page then displays success or error feedback and updates any cart summary indicators using values from the response.
Cart navigation: Clicking a 'View cart' button or cart icon navigates to cart.html so the user can review their cart contents.

Initial page load / URL-driven state restore: On page load or when the URL changes (e.g., via navigation), the page reads categoryId, maxPrice, format, type, isFree, page, and sortBy from the URL and uses these values to configure filters and call getProductFilterOptions and listProductsByCategory so the UI reflects the URL-defined state.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is entirely driven by URL query parameters; no persistent client storage (such as localStorage) is used. On every load of store.html, the application reads categoryId, maxPrice, format, type, isFree, page, and sortBy from the URL. If a parameter is missing, the page uses internal defaults only for that page instance (e.g., default page=1) but does not persist them outside the current URL. The active category is determined from categoryId and is used when calling getProductFilterOptions and listProductsByCategory. The filters object passed to listProductsByCategory is constructed from the URL parameters: maxPrice is mapped to a maximum price filter; format and type are mapped to format and type filters; isFree is parsed from its string representation (for example, 'true' meaning free-only) into a boolean and used to limit results to free resources. The sortBy parameter is mapped to the sort_by parameter for listProductsByCategory. The page parameter controls which segment of results is shown; changing this parameter re-requests the same filtered dataset for a different page. When the user interacts with category navigation, filters, sorting, or pagination, the application updates the URL query string to reflect the new state (for example, replacing or adding parameters) and then re-runs the data loading sequence using the updated parameters. This ensures that the URL always represents the current view and that using the browser's back/forward buttons or sharing the URL restores the same category, filters, sort order, and page. The only state not encoded in the URL is transient UI state such as inline success/error messages or cart indicators, which are derived from the latest addProductToCart response and not persisted between page reloads.

components.functionality: Loads and displays all active resource categories for the store, highlights the currently selected category based on categoryId from the URL, and allows the user to switch categories by updating the categoryId URL parameter.
event_handlers: onCategoryNavInit(loads categories via getStoreCategories and marks the active one using categoryId from URL)
onCategoryClick(updates the categoryId parameter in the URL, optionally resets page to 1, then triggers a refresh of filters and product listings)

components.functionality: Shows filter controls (price range, formats, resource types, and a free-only toggle) for the active category. Initializes its options using getProductFilterOptions and reflects any existing URL parameters. On user changes, it updates the relevant URL parameters and triggers reloading of the product list.
event_handlers: onFilterPanelInit(reads categoryId from URL, calls getProductFilterOptions, initializes control ranges and options based on the response, and sets current values from URL parameters maxPrice, format, type, and isFree)
onMaxPriceChange(updates maxPrice in the URL query string, resets page to 1, and triggers listProductsByCategory with updated filters)
onFormatChange(updates format in the URL query string based on selected format value(s) and triggers listProductsByCategory)
onTypeChange(updates type in the URL query string based on selected resource type and triggers listProductsByCategory)
onFreeToggleChange(sets or clears the isFree parameter in the URL based on the toggle state, resets page to 1, and triggers listProductsByCategory with a free-only filter when enabled)

onClearFilters(optional handler that removes filter-related parameters such as maxPrice, format, type, and isFree from the URL and triggers a reload of listProductsByCategory with no additional filters)

components.functionality: Allows the user to choose how products are ordered (e.g., by price or other supported sort options). Reads and sets the sortBy URL parameter and triggers reloading of the product list.
event_handlers: onSortControlInit(reads sortBy from the URL and sets the initial selected sort option in the control)
onSortOptionChange(updates the sortBy parameter in the URL, resets page to 1, and triggers a new call to listProductsByCategory using the selected sort_by value)

components.functionality: Displays a paginated list of products for the active category with the current filters and sort order applied. Each product entry shows basic information (title, price, format, free/paid status, stock information) and exposes actions to view details and add to cart.
event_handlers: onProductGridInit(reads categoryId, maxPrice, format, type, isFree, sortBy, and page from the URL, constructs the filters object, and calls listProductsByCategory with categoryId, filters, sort_by, page, and a defined page_size; then renders the returned items)
onFilterOrSortStateChange(triggered externally when URL parameters change; re-runs listProductsByCategory with the latest parameters and updates the grid)
onProductTitleClick(navigates to product_detail.html with id set to the clicked product_id and categoryId set to the current categoryId from the URL)
onProductImageClick(navigates to product_detail.html with id set to the clicked product_id and categoryId set to the current categoryId from the URL)

components.functionality: Provides controls to move between pages of products (previous, next, specific page numbers) based on the total_count returned by listProductsByCategory and the configured page_size. Updates the page URL parameter and reloads the product grid on interaction.
event_handlers: onPaginationInit(derives the total number of pages from the total_count returned by the most recent listProductsByCategory call and the page_size, reads the current page from the URL, and initializes the pagination UI)
onPageNumberClick(sets page in the URL to the selected page number and triggers a new listProductsByCategory call using the existing filters and sortBy)
onNextPageClick(increments the current page value in the URL if not on the last page and triggers listProductsByCategory)
onPreviousPageClick(decrements the current page value in the URL if not on the first page and triggers listProductsByCategory)

components.functionality: Provides an 'Add to cart' action for each product in the product grid (and optionally a quantity selector). Calls addProductToCart when used and updates cart-related indicators and messages based on the response.
event_handlers: onAddToCartClick(reads the relevant product_id and quantity for the product, calls addProductToCart with productId and quantity, and handles the response by showing success or error messages and updating a cart summary indicator using cart_item_count and cart_subtotal)
onQuantityChange(updates the quantity that will be passed to addProductToCart for the associated product item, without changing URL parameters)

components.functionality: Displays a minimal summary of the cart state as known from the latest addProductToCart response, such as the number of items in the cart and current subtotal. Provides navigation to cart.html.
event_handlers: onCartSummaryInit(initializes with no counts or uses values derived from the current page state; does not perform additional API calls on its own)
onCartUpdatedFromAddToCart(triggered when addProductToCart returns; updates the displayed cart item count and subtotal using cart_item_count and cart_subtotal)
onViewCartClick(navigates the user to cart.html to review the full cart and begin checkout)

components.functionality: Centralizes reading from and writing to the URL query string so that category selection, filters, sort order, and pagination remain in sync with the UI. Not visible to users, but coordinates data reloads when the URL changes.
event_handlers: onPageLoad(reads categoryId, maxPrice, format, type, isFree, page, and sortBy from the URL and emits an initialization event to other components such as the filter panel, sort control, and product grid)
onUrlChange(listens for changes to the URL via navigation or programmatic updates and notifies dependent components to refresh their state and re-call relevant interfaces)
onStateUpdateRequest(receives requests from components to change part of the state, updates the appropriate URL parameter(s) accordingly, and triggers onUrlChange behavior)

layout.grid_system: Implicit single central column likely mapped to a standard 12-column grid, with all visible content centered within a full-width hero section.
layout.layout_pattern: The store page keeps a single, centered content column sitting on top of a full-width, photographic background that continues the hero aesthetic from the mockup. All store components stack vertically in a clear top‑to‑bottom flow: a page-level header (existing pattern), then the category navigation, then a compact control band (filters, sort, cart), followed by the product grid, and finally pagination. Any “sidebar” behavior (filters) appears as a temporary overlay or slide-out attached to this central column rather than a persistent left/right column, preserving the one-column feel.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: The store page keeps a single, centered content column sitting on top of a full-width, photographic background that continues the hero aesthetic from the mockup. All store components stack vertically in a clear top‑to‑bottom flow: a page-level header (existing pattern), then the category navigation, then a compact control band (filters, sort, cart), followed by the product grid, and finally pagination. Any “sidebar” behavior (filters) appears as a temporary overlay or slide-out attached to this central column rather than a persistent left/right column, preserving the one-column feel.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The upper ~20–25% of the viewport is reserved for the hero/header and category navigation, establishing context and calm. The central ~55–60% is dedicated to the product grid, which occupies a centered content band (about 70–80% of the viewport width) with generous vertical spacing between rows. The remaining vertical space allows the background image and lower gradient to breathe, with pagination and footer elements sitting in the quieter lower portion. Each major band (hero/header, category nav, control band, grid, pagination) is separated by 64–96px of vertical space to maintain the devotional openness.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density stays low and airy. Each cluster (navigation, controls, grid, pagination) is visually contained with 16–32px inner padding and generous margins around it. The number of visible interactive elements in any given horizontal band is kept limited: a small number of category pills, a compact row for filter/sort/cart, and a moderately spaced grid of cards. This approach prevents the store from feeling transactional or cluttered; instead it feels like a curated, contemplative resource library.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first lands near the top on the existing site navigation and store page heading, then is gently drawn to the centered category navigation as the primary wayfinding element. From there, the eye moves downward to the subtle control band (filters, sort, cart summary) and naturally continues into the product grid, which sits over the darker, mid-height region of the background for readability. Pagination rests at the lower, lighter area, visually signaling the end of the content. When the filter sidebar is opened, it slides in from the right of the central column as a soft overlay, temporarily becoming a focal point while still anchored to the same vertical flow.

layout.overall_description: The Store / Resources page feels like an extension of the hero landing aesthetic: a full-bleed sunset or warm-toned background fills the viewport, with a gently darkened overlay behind the central content column for legibility. At the top, the existing navigation and page title sit in a quiet band, followed by a centered row of category links that act as the spiritual “sections” of the library. Below this, a single, slim control band presents filtering, sorting, and cart status in a restrained, balanced row that never reaches the edges of the screen. The main body of the page is a centered product grid, three cards wide on larger screens, each card breathing with generous padding and white space around it. Inline add-to-cart actions are tucked neatly at the bottom of each card, keeping the emphasis on the resource title and description. At the very bottom of the grid, minimalist pagination controls sit centered with ample padding above and below, framing the grid content and letting the background imagery reassert itself toward the footer. The overall impression is of a calm, ordered catalog gently floating over a devotional scene, with every component aligned to a single, reverent vertical axis.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category navigation appears as the first distinct store-specific band beneath the page header/hero, centered horizontally within the main content column. It spans roughly 70–80% of the viewport width on large screens, never touching the edges, and aligns to the same central axis as the hero typography. Category items are presented as evenly spaced text links or pill-style buttons in a single horizontal row that can wrap to two lines if necessary, but the overall block remains centered. There is generous vertical spacing: about 32px of padding above and below the category row, and roughly 64px of space separating it from the header above and the control band below, preserving the open, contemplative feel. On smaller screens, the component maintains its central alignment, allowing category items to wrap while keeping clear margins on the left and right.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block-level element within the central column; scrolls with the page. On smaller screens it remains full-width within the content column but stays vertically compact so it doesn’t dominate the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The filter panel is hidden by default and is revealed when the user activates the filter control in the control band. When opened on larger screens, it slides in from the right edge of the central content column as a vertical panel that overlays the background and slightly overlaps the right side of the product grid, rather than permanently compressing the layout into two columns. This panel occupies roughly 30–35% of the central column’s width and extends from just beneath the control band down toward mid-page, with 24–32px of inner padding on all sides and softly rounded corners or a subtle shadow to distinguish it from the background. The left edge of the panel aligns with the rightmost card column, ensuring it still feels tied to the main column. On smaller screens, instead of a side panel, it drops down from beneath the control band as a full-width, centered block spanning about 90% of the viewport width, pushing the grid down. In both cases, the filter content is arranged in a clear vertical stack with generous spacing between filter groups (16–24px) to maintain low content density.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Temporarily overlaying, slide-in/slide-down panel anchored to the main content column. Hidden by default, it appears above the product grid in the stacking order and can be dismissed to return to the single-column feel. It scrolls with the page once opened if content overflows its height.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The sort control lives in a slim, centered control band directly beneath the category navigation and above the product grid. The band spans approximately 70–80% of the viewport width, aligned with the central column. Within this band, the sort control is positioned toward the right half but not touching the edge of the band, balancing visually against the filter trigger on the left and the cart summary on the far right. Vertically, the band has about 16–24px of padding top and bottom, and a subtle bottom divider or shadow that visually separates it from the product grid below. On smaller screens, the sort control moves beneath the filter trigger in a vertically stacked arrangement while still remaining centered within the same band, preserving a clear, linear scan from filters to sort to cart.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline element within a horizontal control row that scrolls with the page. It adapts responsively: horizontal alignment on wide screens, stacking within the control band on narrow screens while maintaining central alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product grid is the dominant content block and occupies the central portion of the page, sitting directly below the control band with around 32–48px of vertical separation. The grid container is centered and constrained to about 70–80% of the viewport width (e.g., a max-width column), leaving wide, calm margins on both sides through which the background photography is still visible. On larger screens, products are arranged in 2–3 columns depending on width (ideally three), with generous horizontal gutters of 24–32px between cards and vertical spacing of 32–40px between rows. Each card is vertically oriented, with consistent inner padding (24–32px) and plenty of negative space around the text and image, reflecting the site’s meditative tone. The top of the grid aligns visually with the darker mid-region of the background image to maximize contrast, while the lower rows transition into slightly lighter areas, reinforcing depth. On smaller screens, the grid collapses to a single column; cards remain centered with equal left and right padding, preserving the sense of a single, steady reading column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Standard responsive grid within the central column. It scrolls naturally with the page, adjusting the number of columns based on viewport width while preserving central alignment and consistent gutters.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: Pagination controls are placed directly beneath the product grid, with a generous buffer of approximately 32–48px separating them from the last row of cards, and an additional 64–96px below them before the footer or next major section. The pagination sits perfectly centered on the same axis as the grid and navigation, occupying only as much width as needed for the controls themselves (for instance, a compact row of page numbers and next/previous arrows). There is 16–24px of padding above and below the clickable elements to maintain touch-friendly spacing and a light visual footprint. The surrounding whitespace allows the pagination to act as a clear visual “end of content” marker without feeling heavy or cramped.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static, inline element at the bottom of the central column. It scrolls with the page and always appears immediately after the grid content for the current page.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The inline add-to-cart form is embedded within each product card, consistently anchored near the bottom of the card to create a stable, rhythmic grid. Above it sit the product image, title, and brief description; below it there is a small cushion of space (8–16px) to the card’s bottom edge. The button or quantity selector and button are aligned to the center of the card’s width, spanning roughly 60–70% of the card’s inner width, leaving visible margins on either side to preserve the airy feel. Vertical padding around the controls is at least 12–16px, ensuring the action feels deliberate and not crowded. This placement makes the call-to-action visible and accessible without dominating the card, keeping the focus on the resource content first.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within each product card, part of the card’s vertical content flow. It stretches or wraps gracefully on smaller screens but always remains anchored near the bottom of the card for visual consistency.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart summary appears as a compact status indicator within the same control band as the filter and sort controls, aligned to the far right edge of that central band but still inside the 70–80% content width. It might present as a small pill or icon with a subtle numeric badge and possibly a short label (e.g., “Cart (2)”), maintaining the refined, understated aesthetic. There is at least 16px of horizontal space between the cart summary and the sort control to prevent crowding. Vertically, it is centered within the height of the control band and inherits the band’s 16–24px padding. The positioning allows users to notice their cart at a glance after scanning filters and sort, without competing with the category navigation or product grid. On smaller screens, it may move to a top-right position within the control band or sit on a line just below the filters/sort, still right-aligned within the central column.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the control band, optionally sticky on very tall pages: on larger screens it can become a small, right-aligned element that remains visible at the top of the viewport as the user scrolls the product grid, as long as it does not disrupt the central vertical rhythm.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The URL state manager has no visible UI. Conceptually, it sits behind all the visible components, connecting the category navigation, filter panel, sort control, product grid, pagination, and cart summary. As users change categories, toggle filters, adjust sorting, or move between pages, the visible components update while the URL state manager silently synchronizes these states with the URL, ensuring deep links and back/forward navigation behave intuitively. Its presence is only inferred from the consistency of the page’s behavior, not from any visual element.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Non-visual, background behavior. It does not occupy any layout space or affect the spatial arrangement of visible components; it simply listens to and updates state as the user interacts with the page.

name: Product Detail
title: Product Detail
description: Displays full details for a single resource (book, devotional, media) and lets the user add it to their cart or library, with navigation back to its store category.

core_features: Load and display full product information based on the `id` URL parameter using the `getProductDetail` interface, including title, description, price, free/paid status, format, type, and category name.

Indicate the product format and type (e.g., physical, ebook, audio, digital_download; book, devotional, curriculum, merchandise, other) and whether it is a shippable physical item by using `Product.format` and `Product.is_physical_shippable`.
Display correct pricing information, including a clearly labeled free state when `Product.is_free` is true and the numeric price from `Product.price`.
Provide purchasing or saving controls: an "Add to Cart" button for paid items and an "Add to Library" button for free items, both implemented via the `addProductToCart` interface.

Handle quantity selection for products (typically physical items) using `Product.stock_quantity` when available, including disabling or constraining quantity selection when stock or status prevents ordering.
Show status-dependent behaviors based on `Product.status` (e.g., disable purchase actions if the product is not active).
Provide a context-aware "Back to Category" link that navigates to `store.html` using either the incoming `categoryId` URL parameter or, if absent, the `Product.category_id` from `getProductDetail`.
After adding a product via `addProductToCart`, display feedback using the returned `success`, `message`, `cart_item_count`, and `cart_subtotal`, and present navigation options to `cart.html`.
Enable direct access via shared URLs (e.g., `product_detail.html?id=<productId>`), correctly initializing state from the URL without relying on stored client state.
user_workflows: View a product from the store listing: (1) User clicks a product in `store.html`, which links to `product_detail.html?id=<productId>&categoryId=<categoryId>`. (2) Product Detail reads the `id` (and optional `categoryId`) from the URL. (3) The page calls `getProductDetail(productId=id)` and shows a loading state. (4) On success, the page displays the product title, description, price or free label, format, and related metadata. (5) The user can review details and either add the product to cart/library or go back to the category listing using the "Back to Category" link that points to `store.html?categoryId=<originalOrDerivedCategoryId>`.

Open a product from the cart: (1) User clicks a product title or image in `cart.html`, which links to `product_detail.html?id=<productId>`. (2) Product Detail reads `id` from the URL and calls `getProductDetail(productId=id)`. (3) The user reviews the product information. (4) If desired, the user adds another quantity to the cart via the add-to-cart controls. (5) After a successful add, the page shows updated cart summary from the `addProductToCart` response, and the user can click "View Cart" to return to `cart.html`.

Add a paid product to cart: (1) With a paid product loaded, the user checks that the product is available (status active, sufficient stock if provided). (2) The user selects a quantity (default of 1, constrained by any available `stock_quantity`). (3) The user clicks the "Add to Cart" button. (4) The page validates quantity input and disables the button while the request is in progress. (5) The page calls `addProductToCart(productId=product.id, quantity=<selectedQuantity>)`. (6) On success, the form re-enables, a success notification is shown using `message`, and the UI updates any inline cart count and subtotal using `cart_item_count` and `cart_subtotal`. (7) The user may click "View Cart" to navigate to `cart.html`.

Add a free resource to library: (1) With a free product (where `is_free` is true) loaded, the purchase panel labels the main action as "Add to Library" instead of "Add to Cart". (2) The user clicks "Add to Library" (quantity may be fixed to 1 or handled according to site rules). (3) The page calls `addProductToCart(productId=product.id, quantity=1)` while disabling the action button. (4) On success, the page displays a confirmation message and uses `cart_item_count` and `cart_subtotal` from the response to reflect any changes to the cart state (even for free items). (5) The user may proceed to `cart.html` if they intend to check out or access free digital resources through whatever flow is implemented downstream.

Navigate back to category: (1) After viewing details, the user clicks "Back to Category". (2) If the page was loaded with `categoryId` in the URL, the link navigates to `store.html?categoryId=<thatIncomingCategoryId>`. (3) If `categoryId` was not provided, the page uses `product.category_id` from `getProductDetail` to navigate to `store.html?categoryId=<product.category_id>`. (4) If neither value is available (e.g., due to an error), the back-to-category control is disabled or omitted to avoid broken navigation.

Handle product loading errors: (1) If `id` is missing from the URL, the page does not call `getProductDetail` and instead shows a functional error state indicating that a product identifier is required. (2) If `getProductDetail` fails or returns an invalid/unsupported product (e.g., null, inactive status depending on business rules), the page shows an error message and disables add-to-cart/library interactions. (3) The "Back to Category" link still attempts to use any provided `categoryId` to allow the user to return to browsing.
interactions: On initial page load or when the `id` URL parameter changes, trigger a call to `getProductDetail` and update the page state to represent loading, success, or error.
When the user changes the quantity field, update internal state to keep the selected quantity within allowed bounds (e.g., minimum 1; optional maximum based on `Product.stock_quantity` if present).

When the user clicks the primary action button ("Add to Cart" for paid or "Add to Library" for free), validate the current product state (status and any stock rules) and call `addProductToCart` with the appropriate `productId` and `quantity`.
During an `addProductToCart` request, disable the action button and quantity input to prevent duplicate submissions; re-enable them after a response is received.

On successful `addProductToCart` response, display the returned `message`, optionally display `cart_item_count` and `cart_subtotal`, and expose navigation actions to `cart.html` (e.g., "View Cart" or "Proceed to Checkout").
On unsuccessful `addProductToCart` response (where `success` is false), display the `message` as an error and keep the user on the same page with purchase controls enabled for retry.

When the user clicks "Back to Category", read the current category context (either the original URL `categoryId` or the `Product.category_id`) and navigate to `store.html?categoryId=<resolvedCategoryId>`.
If the user clicks "View Cart" from a success notification, navigate directly to `cart.html` without altering URL parameters.
If any error occurs while loading or adding to cart (e.g., interface error or invalid data), show a clear error message and prevent actions that depend on missing data.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven entirely by URL parameters and the results of the `getProductDetail` and `addProductToCart` interfaces, without using localStorage or other persistent client storage. On load, the page parses the `id` and optional `categoryId` from the query string (e.g., `product_detail.html?id=<productId>&categoryId=<categoryId>`). If `id` is missing or empty, the page enters an 'invalid parameter' state and does not call `getProductDetail`. If `id` is valid, the page sets a 'loading' state and calls `getProductDetail(productId=id)`. When the response arrives, the page transitions to either a 'ready' state (with a bound `Product` and `category_name`) or an 'error' state if the call fails or returns unusable data. The `categoryId` URL parameter, if provided, is stored in in-memory page state as the 'incomingCategoryId'. This value takes precedence when constructing the 'Back to Category' URL: `store.html?categoryId=<incomingCategoryId>`. If `incomingCategoryId` is not present, the page falls back to `product.category_id` from the `getProductDetail` response to construct `store.html?categoryId=<product.category_id>`. If neither is available (e.g., due to an error), the back link is hidden or disabled. The page also maintains transient state for the selected quantity, whether the add-to-cart action is in progress, and the last add-to-cart result (success, message, cart_item_count, cart_subtotal). These states are reset whenever the `id` URL parameter changes. Navigation to `cart.html` from this page does not require query parameters; it is a straightforward redirect. No cart or product selection information is stored in the browser beyond the current in-memory state of the Product Detail view.

components.functionality: Initializes the Product Detail page by reading URL parameters, calling `getProductDetail`, and managing global loading and error states before rendering child components.
event_handlers: onPageLoad: Parse `id` and `categoryId` from the current URL query string. If `id` is absent or invalid, set `page_state.errorMessage` and `page_state.loadingStatus='error'` and skip the API call. If `id` is valid, set `page_state.loadingStatus='loading'`, store `incomingCategoryId` from the URL if present, and call `getProductDetail(productId=id)`.

onGetProductDetailSuccess: When `getProductDetail` resolves, store the returned `product` and `category_name` into the page's data model, set `page_state.loadingStatus='ready'`, and clear any previous error message.

onGetProductDetailError: If `getProductDetail` fails or returns unusable data, set `page_state.loadingStatus='error'`, populate `page_state.errorMessage` with a functional description, and avoid rendering purchase and back-link components that depend on valid product data.
onUrlChange: If the application supports in-page navigation that modifies the `id` parameter without a full reload, re-run the onPageLoad logic to fetch and display the new product.

components.functionality: Displays essential product summary information at the top of the page, including title, optional image, category name, format/type indicators, and free/paid status.
event_handlers: onRender: If `page_state.loadingStatus` is 'loading', render a loading indicator instead of product data. If 'error', render the error message. If 'ready', render product summary fields bound to `Product` and `category_name`.

onProductChange: When the bound `Product` changes (e.g., after a new `getProductDetail` call), update displayed title, format/type labels, optional image, and free/paid label based on `Product.is_free` and `Product.price`.

components.functionality: Shows detailed product information such as full description and any other descriptive fields sourced from the Product model.
event_handlers: onRender: Display detailed fields (e.g., `Product.description` and related metadata) when `page_state.loadingStatus` is 'ready'. If loading, show a placeholder; if error or no product, hide this section.
onProductChange: When a new Product is loaded, update the description and any format/type-specific explanatory text derived from `Product.format`, `Product.type`, and `Product.is_physical_shippable`.

components.functionality: Provides the main purchase/save controls: quantity selection (when applicable) and a context-aware button labeled 'Add to Cart' for paid items or 'Add to Library' for free items, and calls `addProductToCart` with the chosen quantity.
data_binding: addToCartState: { selectedQuantity, isSubmitting, lastResult: { success, message, cart_item_count, cart_subtotal } }
event_handlers: onInit: When `page_state.loadingStatus` switches to 'ready', initialize `addToCartState.selectedQuantity` to a default (for example, 1) and clear any prior `addToCartState.lastResult`.

onChangeQuantity: When the user changes the quantity field, validate that the value is a positive integer and, if `Product.stock_quantity` is present, optionally ensure the quantity does not exceed `Product.stock_quantity`. If invalid, prevent submission and show inline validation feedback.

onClickPrimaryAction: When the user clicks the primary action button ('Add to Cart' if `Product.is_free` is false, 'Add to Library' if `Product.is_free` is true), perform client-side validation: ensure `Product` is loaded, `Product.status` allows ordering, quantity is valid, and any additional rules are satisfied. If validation passes, set `addToCartState.isSubmitting=true`, disable the button and quantity input, and call `addProductToCart(productId=Product.id, quantity=addToCartState.selectedQuantityOr1ForFree)`. The label of the button is determined purely by `Product.is_free`.

onAddProductToCartSuccess: When `addProductToCart` returns with `success=true`, set `addToCartState.isSubmitting=false`, store the full response object in `addToCartState.lastResult`, and re-enable controls. The panel uses `lastResult.message` for a success message, and `lastResult.cart_item_count` and `lastResult.cart_subtotal` for cart summary display.

onAddProductToCartErrorOrFailure: When `addProductToCart` fails (e.g., network error) or returns `success=false`, set `addToCartState.isSubmitting=false`, store the response (if any) in `addToCartState.lastResult`, and show `lastResult.message` as an error. Keep the quantity field and button enabled for user retry.

onProductUnavailable: If `Product.status` is not 'active' or `Product.stock_quantity` indicates zero availability under the chosen business rules, disable the primary action button and quantity field, and display a state-specific message instead of allowing calls to `addProductToCart`.

components.functionality: Displays feedback after an `addProductToCart` operation, including success or error messages and cart summary information, and provides navigation options to view the cart or continue browsing.
event_handlers: onAddToCartResultChange: When `addToCartState.lastResult` changes, determine whether to show the banner. If `lastResult` is null, hide it. If present, show `lastResult.message` and distinguish between success and error states by `lastResult.success`.
onClickViewCart: When the user clicks a 'View Cart' or 'Proceed to Checkout' control in the banner after a successful add, navigate to `cart.html` (no additional parameters required).
onDismissNotification: When the user closes the banner, clear `addToCartState.lastResult` to hide the notification.

components.functionality: Provides a 'Back to Category' navigation control that routes the user back to the appropriate store listing based on either the incoming URL parameter or the product's category.
event_handlers: onRender: Once `page_state.loadingStatus` is 'ready', compute a resolved `targetCategoryId` by using `page_state.incomingCategoryId` if it is defined and non-empty; otherwise, use `Product.category_id` if available. If `targetCategoryId` is not available due to an error, hide or disable the back-link control.
onClickBackToCategory: When the user clicks the control and `targetCategoryId` is available, navigate to `store.html?categoryId=<targetCategoryId>`.

onProductOrCategoryChange: If either `page_state.incomingCategoryId` or `Product.category_id` changes (for example, after a different product is loaded), recompute the back-link target and update the link destination and label (which may include `getProductDetail.category_name` if present).

layout.grid_system: Implicit single central column likely mapped to a standard 12-column grid, with all visible content centered within a full-width hero section.
layout.layout_pattern: All components sit in a single, centered vertical column over a full-width background (photo or soft gradient), mirroring the hero-style landing layout. From top to bottom the order is: category-back-link as a thin navigation strip, product-detail-header as the large devotional title block, cart-notification-banner (when active) tucked just below the header, then the product-info-section as the central reading area, followed by the product-purchase-panel as the primary action block. The page-loader temporarily overlays the entire page when needed. No sidebars or multi-column splits are used; everything is stacked and center-aligned within a constrained content width.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All components sit in a single, centered vertical column over a full-width background (photo or soft gradient), mirroring the hero-style landing layout. From top to bottom the order is: category-back-link as a thin navigation strip, product-detail-header as the large devotional title block, cart-notification-banner (when active) tucked just below the header, then the product-info-section as the central reading area, followed by the product-purchase-panel as the primary action block. The page-loader temporarily overlays the entire page when needed. No sidebars or multi-column splits are used; everything is stacked and center-aligned within a constrained content width.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The upper 20–25% of the viewport is reserved for the back link and product header, echoing the nav + brand area of the hero. The central 40–50% is dedicated to the product-info-section and product-purchase-panel, positioned over the visually calmer or darker part of the background for legibility. The remaining area below is largely open, with generous vertical breathing room to maintain a reverent, uncluttered feel. All main content sits in a central column spanning roughly 60–70% of the viewport width (max content width around 960–1120px), with text blocks padded internally by 16–24px and sections separated vertically by 48–64px, expanding to 64–96px between major groupings (header ↔ body).
layout_strategies.content_density: Content remains deliberately sparse. Each component is given ample whitespace above and below, with only a few major blocks per screenful. Text line lengths are moderate to short, and no component is tightly packed. The product-info-section and product-purchase-panel read as distinct, airy cards or bands within the hero-like field, with inner padding of 24–32px to preserve the devotional calm and avoid any visual crowding typical of commercial product pages.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The eye first catches the subtle navigation cue of the category-back-link at the very top, then immediately drops to the bold, centered product-detail-header which plays the role of the hero title. From there, the gaze flows downward to the product-info-section, which occupies the visual center similar to the scripture block in the mockup. Finally, attention moves to the product-purchase-panel where the primary action (add to cart/library) is offered. When present, the cart-notification-banner appears just under the header, temporarily intercepting attention before the eye continues down the established vertical path. The background remains unobtrusive, framing the central column and gently guiding the eye downward without competing with the content.

layout.overall_description: The product detail page presents as a single, tall devotional hero with all content aligned to a central column floating over a full-width photographic or softly graded background. At the very top, a slim navigation row holds the category back link, quietly anchoring the page. Just below, the product title in an elegant serif stretches across one or two centered lines, occupying the upper quarter of the viewport and echoing a classic ministry masthead. As the user scrolls or glances downward, they enter the spacious middle band where the product description and spiritual context are presented, much like a scripture passage, centered and comfortably padded within the column. Slightly further down, a softly elevated purchase panel appears as a calm, card-like section containing pricing and action controls, aligned with the same central axis. Around all of this, the edges of the screen remain visually light and mostly free of text, allowing the background imagery to frame the content and reinforce a reverent, focused reading experience. When the cart notification appears, it briefly forms a gentle band beneath the header, while the full-screen loader, when active, veils the entire composition with a centered indicator, preserving the clean vertical hierarchy beneath.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The page loader appears as a full-screen veil that sits above all other components. It covers 100% of the viewport width and height, dimming or softly blurring the background hero image and content beneath. At the exact center of the viewport, a small, minimal loading indicator and optional short message are placed within an invisible square roughly 120–160px wide, aligned to the same central vertical axis as the rest of the page content. Generous empty space surrounds this indicator so it feels calm and unobtrusive, rather than busy. When inactive, the loader is completely hidden and does not occupy any space in the layout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Fixed overlay covering the entire viewport; sits above all components using a high stacking order and does not affect underlying layout or scrolling.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product detail header sits near the top of the page, immediately below the thin back-link strip and within the same central content column. It spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width (capped around 960–1120px) and is horizontally centered, with generous left and right margins allowing the background image to frame it. Vertically, it occupies roughly the upper 20–25% of the viewport: 24–32px of whitespace separate it from the back link above, and 32–48px of whitespace separate it from the body content below. The main product title is set in large, centered serif typography, potentially on one or two lines, creating a quiet hero-like masthead. Any subtitle or short metadata lines sit directly beneath, tightly grouped with 8–16px spacing, so the entire header reads as a single, cohesive block on the central axis.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static within the normal document flow; scrolls away with the page. It defines the top of the main content stack but does not stick to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product info section is positioned directly below the product header, separated from it by a generous vertical gap of about 48–64px to maintain the airy, devotional rhythm. It shares the same central column width—around 60–70% of the viewport, max 960–1120px—and is perfectly centered horizontally. Within this section, the text (description, key details, spiritual context) is center-aligned or softly left-aligned within the column but kept within a comfortable text measure (approximately 640–720px) so lines remain easy to read. The block itself has 24–32px of internal padding on all sides, either as invisible breathing room or subtly indicated by a very soft overlay or translucent panel over the background. Vertically, this section is allowed to expand as needed, but on typical viewports its core content sits around the middle third of the screen, analogous to the scripture passage in the original hero, letting the background darken or calm behind it for strong legibility.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static block within the central vertical flow; it expands vertically with content and pushes subsequent components downward while maintaining the central alignment.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The product purchase panel is stacked directly below the product-info-section, with a vertical gap of about 32–48px separating the two, so it feels related but still distinct. It is aligned to the same central axis and uses the same column width of roughly 60–70% of the viewport (max 960–1120px), ensuring no side-by-side layouts or sidebars. The panel itself appears as a softly elevated card or clearly framed band atop the background, with 24–32px of internal padding on all sides to keep controls from feeling cramped. Key purchase details (price, format selection, quantity, primary action button) are arranged in a vertically stacked manner within the panel, centered horizontally, with 8–16px spacing between elements to retain the minimalist, reverent tone. The panel occupies much of the central-to-lower middle portion of the initial viewport, echoing the mockup’s central focal region, and acts as the main actionable destination once the user has read the product information above.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static component in the vertical flow. It does not stick or float but is visually emphasized through contrast, padding, and proximity to the central viewing area.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart notification banner appears as a slim, full-width horizontal band that is visually anchored to the central column rather than the browser edges. When active, it sits just below the product-detail-header and above the product-info-section, effectively inserting itself between them. It spans the same content width as the other components (around 60–70% of the viewport, max 960–1120px) and is centered horizontally, with 16–24px of internal padding vertically to keep the message and icon comfortably spaced. Above and below the banner there is 16–24px of whitespace so it feels like a gentle, momentary overlay of information rather than a cramped alert. The banner’s background color is slightly more vivid than the page background but remains compatible with the warm, spiritual palette, ensuring the message is noticed without breaking the contemplative mood.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Appears and disappears dynamically in the normal document flow, pushing the product-info-section down when visible. Optionally may animate in from the top but remains attached to its position beneath the header rather than fixed to the viewport.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The category back link occupies a thin horizontal strip at the very top of the content area, acting as the page’s primary navigation element in place of a full menu. It is positioned flush near the top edge of the viewport with 16–24px of top margin from the browser window, echoing the subtle top navigation band in the hero mockup. The link itself is left-aligned within the central content width (around 60–70% of the viewport, max 960–1120px), so it begins near the left edge of the central column while still sitting comfortably within the middle of the screen overall. There is 8–16px of vertical padding above and below the text, and ample empty space to the right, keeping this strip visually light and unobtrusive. Below the back link, 16–24px of whitespace creates a clear separation before the bold product-detail-header begins, reinforcing the top-to-bottom visual hierarchy.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: Static element at the top of the page’s main content stack; it scrolls away with the content and does not remain fixed but consistently marks the starting point of the vertical flow.

name: Cart & Checkout
title: Cart & Checkout
description: Allows users to review the contents of their cart, update or remove items, select shipping, enter shipping and payment details, place an order, and view an order confirmation summary.

core_features: Load the current cart, including items, subtotal, physical-item flag, free-shipping eligibility, and any applied shipping option, using getCartSummary.
Display all cart items with quantities, unit prices, line totals, and whether each item is free or requires shipping, and allow navigation to the product detail page from each item.
Allow inline updating of cart item quantities and automatic recalculation of cart totals and free-shipping eligibility using updateCartItemQuantity.
Allow removing items from the cart and immediately refresh the cart contents, subtotal, and free-shipping eligibility using removeCartItem.

Show order subtotal, current shipping cost (if already chosen), and the combined estimated total; display whether the cart is eligible_for_free_shipping using data from getCartSummary, and if not eligible, show how much more is needed based on free_shipping_min_order_total without hardcoding a threshold.

If the cart has_physical_items is true, load available shipping options (including any free_shipping option and eligibility flags) using getAvailableShippingOptionsForCart and allow users to select one, calling setCartShippingOption and updating shipping_cost and total.
Provide a checkout form that collects required shipping address, contact information, and payment details, and submit these to placeOrderForCurrentCart to place the order for the current cart.

Handle both carts that include physical, shippable items and carts that contain only non-physical items by conditionally showing or hiding the shipping options selector while still collecting required shipping/contact details.
