After a successful order placement, display an order confirmation summary using the Order and OrderItem data returned by placeOrderForCurrentCart, including order number, item list, shipping summary, and totals, and provide navigation to continue shopping in the store or return to the home page.
Support an 'empty cart' state when getCartSummary returns no items, with a clear path to continue shopping in the store.
user_workflows: Workflow: Review cart from store or product page and continue shopping without checking out: (1) User arrives at cart.html, optionally with a returnTo parameter. (2) Page calls getCartSummary and renders the cart items (if any) and summary. (3) User may adjust item quantities or remove items, triggering updateCartItemQuantity or removeCartItem; each call returns an updated cart summary that replaces the current view. (4) User clicks the 'Continue shopping' control in the cart summary, and the page navigates to store.html with categoryId set to all_resources.

Workflow: Review cart and complete checkout with physical items: (1) User opens cart.html and getCartSummary loads cart contents; if has_physical_items is true, the page may prefetch shipping options via getAvailableShippingOptionsForCart. (2) User adjusts quantities or removes items; after each update, the updated cart summary is shown, including refreshed eligible_for_free_shipping and free_shipping_min_order_total. (3) User clicks 'Proceed to checkout', which switches the page state from cart view to checkout view. (4) In checkout view, if has_physical_items is true, the page calls getAvailableShippingOptionsForCart (if not already loaded) and displays the returned options. (5) User selects a shipping option; this calls setCartShippingOption with shippingOptionId, which returns updated subtotal, shipping_cost, and total, which are bound to the order summary in the checkout form. (6) User fills in shipping name, address, email, phone, payment method, card details, and billing zip. (7) User submits the checkout form; the page calls placeOrderForCurrentCart with the collected shipping and payment fields. (8) On success, the page transitions to an order confirmation view using the Order and OrderItem data returned.

Workflow: Checkout with digital-only items (no physical shipping selection): (1) User opens cart.html; getCartSummary returns items and has_physical_items is false. (2) Cart view is shown without shipping options, but the subtotal and total (which may be equal) are displayed. (3) User clicks 'Proceed to checkout', entering checkout view. (4) Because has_physical_items is false, the shipping options selector is not displayed and getAvailableShippingOptionsForCart is not required for the user interface, while the order summary continues to rely on totals from getCartSummary and any previously applied shipping option, if present. (5) User fills required shipping/contact fields and payment fields (validation rules may depend on total, but no hardcoded assumptions are made). (6) User submits the form, invoking placeOrderForCurrentCart. (7) On success, the order confirmation view is displayed as in other flows.

Workflow: View product details from cart: (1) User views the cart.html page with cart items loaded via getCartSummary. (2) User clicks on a cart item title. (3) The cart page navigates to product_detail.html with id set to the clicked item's product_id.

Workflow: Order confirmation navigation: (1) After placeOrderForCurrentCart returns a success result, the confirmation view shows order details and the returnTo value (if present) is read from the current URL. (2) User can click 'Continue shopping' to go to store.html with categoryId set to all_resources. (3) Alternatively, user can click 'Back to home' to navigate to index.html. (4) The presence and value of returnTo can be used to emphasize either continuing to the store (for example, if returnTo indicates originating from the store) or going back home, without changing the target URLs.
interactions: On initial page load or on URL change, the cart-page controller reads the returnTo URL parameter (if present) and calls getCartSummary to retrieve the current cart contents and summary data.

When the user changes the quantity of an item in the cart (e.g., via a numeric input or stepper), the cart-item list component calls updateCartItemQuantity with the cartItemId and new quantity; the response's cart object (items, subtotal, eligible_for_free_shipping) replaces the current cart state.

When the user removes an item from the cart, the cart-item list component calls removeCartItem with the cartItemId; if success is true, the returned cart replaces the current cart state; if the cart becomes empty, the empty-cart state is shown.

When the user clicks 'Proceed to checkout' from the cart summary, an internal page state variable switches from cart view to checkout view (this may optionally be reflected in a URL query parameter); no API call is required at this moment beyond any existing cart summary.

When the checkout view loads and has_physical_items from getCartSummary is true, the shipping-options selector component calls getAvailableShippingOptionsForCart to retrieve a list of shipping options; the options are displayed with names, descriptions, costs, and estimated delivery ranges.

When the user selects a shipping option in the checkout view, the shipping-options selector calls setCartShippingOption with the chosen shippingOptionId; the response's subtotal, shipping_cost, and total values are bound to the checkout order summary so the user sees the updated total before submitting payment.

When the user fills out the checkout form and submits it, the checkout-form component validates required fields client-side (without hardcoded business rules about amounts) and then calls placeOrderForCurrentCart with the collected shipping, contact, and payment fields; while awaiting a response, the submit control is disabled or a loading state is shown.

If placeOrderForCurrentCart returns success as true, the page switches to the confirmation view and displays the Order and OrderItem data (order number, totals, shipping and contact info, and purchased items). If success is false, the message field is displayed as an error and the user remains on the checkout view.
When the user clicks 'Continue shopping' from either the cart view or the order confirmation view, the page navigates to store.html with categoryId set to all_resources.
When the user clicks 'Back to home' from the order confirmation view, the page navigates to index.html.
When the user clicks a product title in the cart item list, the page navigates to product_detail.html with id set to that item's product_id.
page_functionality.state_logic: The page state is driven by URL parameters and live data from the assigned interfaces, without using localStorage. The primary incoming URL parameter is returnTo, which indicates the calling context (for example, arriving from the store or from a product detail page). On page initialization, the page reads returnTo from the query string of cart.html and keeps it in memory for the current session; it is used to (a) decide which confirmation navigation control (continue shopping vs back to home) is emphasized after order completion, and (b) inform any analytics or contextual messaging without changing the allowed navigation targets. The cart content and pricing state is always sourced from getCartSummary and never hardcoded. When users update or remove items, the page state is updated exclusively from the responses of updateCartItemQuantity and removeCartItem. When users select a shipping option, the current shipping and total amounts are updated exclusively using the response from setCartShippingOption. When the user reaches checkout, the existence of physical items is determined by the has_physical_items field in the getCartSummary response; if true, the shipping-options selector is shown and getAvailableShippingOptionsForCart is called to populate options, otherwise the selector is hidden. After placeOrderForCurrentCart is called and returns successfully, the page state transitions into a confirmation mode and binds its display to the returned Order and OrderItem data; the original cart is treated as checked out according to backend behavior and no longer used for editing. If the implementation needs to support deep-linking directly to the checkout or confirmation views, an additional query parameter (for example, step=cart|checkout|confirmation) can be introduced and controlled by in-page navigation; however, all behavioral decisions still rely on live interface data and not on persisted client storage.

components.functionality: Coordinates loading of the current cart, manages which view is active (cart, checkout, or confirmation), interprets the returnTo URL parameter, and orchestrates calls to getCartSummary, getAvailableShippingOptionsForCart, setCartShippingOption, updateCartItemQuantity, removeCartItem, and placeOrderForCurrentCart. It holds the canonical in-memory state for the cart, shipping options, and the last placed order.

Displays the list of items in the current cart with product title, format, type, quantity, unit_price, total_price, and indicators for free or shippable items. Provides inline controls to change quantity or remove items and exposes product title links to navigate to product_detail.html. It receives its items from the cart-page controller (which uses getCartSummary) and notifies the controller when updates or removals are requested.

Shows when getCartSummary (or the result of updateCartItemQuantity/removeCartItem) yields a cart with no items. It informs the user that the cart is empty and provides a control that navigates to the store so the user can add resources.

Shows the current subtotal, any applied shipping cost (if available from getCartSummary or setCartShippingOption), eligibility for free shipping, and the estimated total. It offers primary actions such as 'Proceed to checkout' and 'Continue shopping'. It relies on values such as subtotal, eligible_for_free_shipping, free_shipping_min_order_total, and applied_shipping_option from getCartSummary or subsequent updates.

Reads subtotal, eligible_for_free_shipping, and free_shipping_min_order_total from the current cart summary and displays whether free shipping has been achieved or how much additional order value is needed. This component never hardcodes any threshold and strictly uses dynamic values from getCartSummary.

In checkout view, if has_physical_items is true, this component retrieves available shipping options for the current cart using getAvailableShippingOptionsForCart, shows each option's name, description, cost, eligibility, and estimated delivery time, and allows the user to select one. On selection, it calls setCartShippingOption with the chosen shippingOptionId and uses the returned subtotal, shipping_cost, and total to update the order summary in the checkout form.

Collects the user's shipping name and address, email, phone, and payment details (paymentMethod, cardNumber, cardExpMonth, cardExpYear, cardCvv, billingZip). It shows a read-only order summary (subtotal, shipping_cost, total) based on the latest cart and shipping state. On submit, it calls placeOrderForCurrentCart with the collected fields. If successful, it passes the returned Order and OrderItem data to the cart-page controller to enter confirmation view; otherwise it displays the message as a validation or error notice.

Displays an order summary after a successful call to placeOrderForCurrentCart. It shows key fields from the Order (such as order_number, status, subtotal, shipping_cost, tax if present, total, shipping and contact information, and shipping_option_id reference) and lists purchased items using OrderItem data. It provides actions to continue shopping in the store and to go back to the home page. It may use the returnTo URL parameter to emphasize one of these navigation paths while always using the outgoing connections defined for store.html and index.html.

layout.grid_system: Implicit single central column likely mapped to a standard 12-column grid, with all visible content centered within a full-width hero section.
layout.layout_pattern: All cart and checkout content is arranged in a single, vertically stacked central column, echoing the hero layout of the mockup. The column is centered within a full-bleed background image and constrained to a comfortable reading width (about 60–70% of the viewport width on large screens, max-width ~960px, with 16–24px padding on smaller screens). The page is state-based: the page controller swaps between (1) a cart/checkout state (free shipping indicator, cart list, summary, shipping options, checkout form) and (2) an order confirmation state (confirmation panel centered in the mid-height of the page). Empty-cart state replaces the cart/checkout stack when there are no items. No sidebars are used; all components align to the same central axis.

layout_strategies.content_arrangement: All cart and checkout content is arranged in a single, vertically stacked central column, echoing the hero layout of the mockup. The column is centered within a full-bleed background image and constrained to a comfortable reading width (about 60–70% of the viewport width on large screens, max-width ~960px, with 16–24px padding on smaller screens). The page is state-based: the page controller swaps between (1) a cart/checkout state (free shipping indicator, cart list, summary, shipping options, checkout form) and (2) an order confirmation state (confirmation panel centered in the mid-height of the page). Empty-cart state replaces the cart/checkout stack when there are no items. No sidebars are used; all components align to the same central axis.
layout_strategies.space_allocation: The top ~20% of the viewport is reserved for the page-level header (handled by the page controller: page title in serif, optional subtle cross/brand echo) and the free-shipping indicator, allowing the background image to remain visible and reverent. The central ~50–60% of the viewport is used for the functional content stack: cart list, summary, shipping options, and checkout form, with each component treated as a distinct card or block separated by 32–48px of vertical space. The bottom ~20–30% remains visually lighter, allowing the background photograph and gradient to show beneath the content and preserving breathing room. On the confirmation view, the confirmation panel is vertically centered in the mid-height band similarly to the scripture block in the original hero.
layout_strategies.content_density: Content density remains low and calm despite the functional nature of the page. Each component appears as a spacious, lightly elevated card with 24–32px internal padding. Vertical spacing between major blocks follows the 32–64px rhythm, preventing crowding between list, summary, and form. Form fields are stacked in a single column with 16–24px vertical spacing between groups, mirroring the devotional, airy feel. There are no dense side-by-side clusters; even where related information is grouped, it respects the single-column, contemplative spacing pattern.
layout_strategies.visual_flow: The visual flow remains strongly vertical. The eye moves from the top header/title area (serif, possibly with a small cross motif) down to the free-shipping indicator (a soft but noticeable status ribbon), then to the cart item list where the main decision-making happens. From there, attention naturally travels downward to the cart summary panel (totals), then to the shipping options selector, and finally to the checkout form where the main call to action sits. On order confirmation, the flow is simplified: after the fixed global header, the eye is drawn directly to a centrally placed confirmation panel with a prominent, reverent heading and summary, similar in placement and weight to the original scripture block. Throughout, the background gradient and generous whitespace guide the eye from top lightness to a slightly darker, more focused central band around the active content.

layout.overall_description: The Cart & Checkout page sits on the same full-bleed, warm sunset background as the hero: a contemplative photograph stretching edge-to-edge, with a gentle top-to-bottom gradient that is lighter near the top and slightly deeper around the page’s center. A global navigation and brand header occupy the very top, and directly below it, the page controller introduces a centered page heading in an elegant serif, echoing the devotional hero title. Beneath this, aligned to the central axis, a subtle free-shipping status ribbon spans most of the content width. As you scroll or glance downward, a vertical stack of light, card-like panels appears: first the cart items, then a padded summary block, followed by a clean shipping options selector and a generous checkout form. Each panel shares the same width, padding, and centered alignment, floating gently against the photographic backdrop while leaving ample margins on all sides so the image still frames the experience. In the order confirmation state, all these functional blocks disappear and are replaced by a single, spacious confirmation panel positioned so that its main message sits around the visual center of the viewport—much like the scripture passage in the original mockup—creating a reverent, concluded feeling to the purchase journey.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-page-controller acts as the structural scaffold for the entire view, anchoring all components to a single, centered column that runs from just below the global navigation down to slightly above the bottom of the viewport. It defines a central content column that spans roughly 60–70% of the viewport width on desktop (max-width around 960px) and shrinks to nearly full width on mobile with 16–24px side padding. At the top of this column, it reserves a band of about 15–20% of the viewport height for the page heading—set in a serif style that visually echoes the hero title—and any subtle devotional iconography. Directly beneath this heading area, it inserts either: (a) the cart/checkout stack (free-shipping-indicator, cart-item-list, cart-summary-panel, shipping-options-selector, checkout-form) in that strict top-to-bottom order, or (b) the order-confirmation-panel alone, centered vertically. In the case of an empty cart, the controller hides the normal stack and centers the empty-cart-state in the middle of the viewport within the same column. It maintains consistent vertical gaps of 32–48px between components, ensuring the stack feels open and aligned to the single-column hero pattern.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The page controller occupies the full scrollable height of the main content area and is not itself visible; it orchestrates which child components are rendered while maintaining a fixed central column layout. It scrolls with the page and does not use sticky or floating behavior, instead relying on the static hero-style stacking of content.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-item-list appears in the upper half of the main content stack when there are items in the cart. It is positioned directly beneath the free-shipping-indicator with a vertical gap of roughly 32–40px, aligning perfectly to the central column. The list is contained within a soft, card-like block that spans about 70% of the viewport width on large screens (matching the other panels), with rounded corners and 24–32px of internal padding. On mobile, it stretches nearly full width with 16–24px side padding while remaining centered. Each cart item is arranged in a vertical sub-stack with generous vertical spacing and a single-column presentation (image, title, quantity controls, and price arranged top-to-bottom or with subtle inline alignment), so the block retains the low-density, contemplative feel rather than looking like a dense table. The list’s overall height will typically occupy a band from just below the top third of the viewport downwards; if it grows taller than the viewport, it simply extends downward, with the overall column scrollable.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The cart-item-list is a standard flow element within the central column; it expands vertically as needed based on item count and scrolls with the page. It does not stick or float. When the cart is empty, this component is removed from the layout entirely by the page controller.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The empty-cart-state occupies the same central column as the rest of the content but is shown only when there are no items in the cart. It is positioned so that its main message (e.g., an empty state illustration and a gentle, devotional-style message) appears roughly at the vertical center of the viewport, similar to where the scripture passage sits in the hero reference. The block spans about 50–60% of the viewport width on large screens (slightly narrower than the cart list for a more intimate feel) and is centered horizontally, with ample whitespace above and below—at least 64px to the top of the column heading and 64px to the bottom of the viewport’s visible area. Inside, it uses 24–32px of padding, with the primary message, scripture-like encouragement, and a call-to-action button stacked vertically and center-aligned. The surrounding photographic background remains clearly visible, framing this minimal content and reinforcing the sense of openness.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The empty-cart-state is a standard, non-sticky block that replaces the entire cart/checkout stack when active. It is vertically centered within the available viewport where possible, but if content height grows (e.g., translations or longer copy), it gracefully overflows and scrolls while maintaining central alignment and generous spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The cart-summary-panel sits directly beneath the cart-item-list, separated by a vertical gap of about 32px to maintain clear hierarchy. It aligns to the same central column and shares the same width as the cart list—about 70% of the viewport width on desktop, limited by the global max-width and center-aligned, and nearly full width with side padding on smaller screens. The panel is styled as a compact but airy card with 24–32px of internal padding. Inside, subtotal, shipping estimate placeholder, tax, and total are stacked vertically in a single column, with each line given 8–16px spacing, avoiding dense table formats. The total amount is slightly emphasized but still presented reverently rather than aggressively. The panel’s overall height is modest, occupying a narrow band so that, at a glance, users can see the bottom of the cart list above and the start of the next component (shipping options) below, reinforcing the linear flow of review → summary → options → checkout.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The cart-summary-panel behaves as a normal block within the vertical flow, expanding slightly in height as more summary rows are added. It scrolls with the page and does not remain fixed; its prominence comes from its placement immediately after the items and before shipping options, not from any special positioning.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The free-shipping-indicator is a slim yet noticeable status ribbon positioned near the top of the content column, directly below the page heading area created by the page controller. It sits within the central column, spanning about 80% of the column width on large screens (slightly wider than the cards below to feel like a banner), with 12–16px of vertical padding and 16–24px of horizontal padding. There is a vertical gap of roughly 24px between the heading and this indicator, and about 16–24px between the indicator and the top of the cart-item-list, allowing it to be clearly distinguished but not isolated. Its text is center-aligned, using a calm sans-serif, and the background uses a warm, golden tone that harmonizes with the sunset palette, signaling encouragement (e.g., “You’re $X away from free shipping”) without overwhelming the devotional aesthetic. The ribbon floats visually against the background but remains light enough not to compete with the main content blocks below.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The free-shipping-indicator is an inline, non-sticky element within the central column. It does not stay fixed on scroll; instead, it scrolls away as the user moves down through the cart and checkout, reflecting the gentle, non-intrusive nature of the design.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The shipping-options-selector is positioned beneath the cart-summary-panel with a vertical gap of about 32px, following the linear path from items to cost overview to shipping method. It is presented as a card that shares the same width and alignment as the summary and cart list—centered in the column, spanning about 70% of the viewport width on large screens and stretching to nearly full width on small screens with 16–24px side padding. The card interior is organized in a single column with 24–32px of padding: a clear, understated heading at the top, followed by radio-style shipping options stacked vertically with 12–16px spacing between each option. Any explanatory text (e.g., delivery windows or devotional phrasing like “Receive your resources by…”) wraps onto multiple lines but maintains the centered, calm rhythm of the page. Overall, the component occupies a modest vertical space, ensuring the start of the checkout form is visible below, reinforcing the directionality of the flow.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The shipping-options-selector is a normal flow component in the stack. It expands in height as needed based on the number of shipping options and scrolls with the page. It does not have sticky behavior and remains visually tied to the summary above and the checkout form below through consistent spacing.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The checkout-form is the most substantial card in the stack and is placed directly beneath the shipping-options-selector, separated by about 40–48px to signal a transition into the final, committed step. It aligns to the same central column and shares the same width—around 70% of the viewport on desktop, capped at the global max-width and centered, and nearly full width on mobile with consistent side padding. Inside the form, content is arranged in a single vertical column with clear grouping: shipping address block, contact details, payment information, and an order review checkbox or final consent—each group separated by 24–32px of vertical spacing. Individual form fields within groups are stacked with 16–24px spacing, avoiding dense multi-column layouts to preserve readability and calm. Labels are left-aligned within the card but visually balanced in the overall centered column. At the bottom, the primary “Place Order” button is centered horizontally and is surrounded by generous whitespace (24–32px above and below), echoing the prominence and centered nature of the scripture block’s call to reflection. The entire form may extend below the initial viewport height; scrolling reveals additional fields while maintaining the feeling of one continuous, central path.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The checkout-form is a standard scrolling block; it grows vertically depending on the number of fields. The user scrolls down through the form; nothing is fixed within it. On submission and successful order placement, this entire block is removed from the layout by the page controller and replaced by the order-confirmation-panel.

component_layouts.layout_narrative: The order-confirmation-panel appears after a successful checkout and replaces the entire cart/checkout stack. It is positioned within the central column so that its primary confirmation message—such as “Thank you for your order” in a serif style—sits roughly at the vertical center of the viewport, mirroring the placement of the scripture text in the original hero. The panel spans about 50–60% of the viewport width on desktop for a more intimate, devotional feel, and remains centered with 16–24px side padding on smaller screens. Inside, the content is stacked vertically: a prominent heading at the top, followed by a brief, calm message (possibly with a scripture verse or blessing), then a summarized order overview and any next-step actions (view order details, continue browsing). These sections are separated by 16–24px of vertical spacing, with the whole panel padded generously (24–32px) within a subtle card that gently lifts from the background. The surrounding areas above and below the panel show more of the sunset photograph, framing the confirmation as a moment of completion and gratitude.
component_layouts.spatial_behavior: The order-confirmation-panel is a single, non-sticky block that is vertically centered within the viewport where possible. If the order details section becomes long, the panel naturally extends downward and the page becomes scrollable, but the initial fold still presents the main confirmation message in the mid-height of the hero area. It replaces the cart and checkout components entirely in this state, maintaining a focused, meditative layout.
